text: I've been seeing a lot of praise for Olivia's new album with very little critique at all so i wanted to share ny thoughts because my view seems to be very different. I see many saying things such as 'this album is good for what it is' but I do believe this album wasn't even that good for what it was supposed to be. The concept had a lot more potential, but jumps right from drop dead girl crush to stupid song and it really has too much sad ballads which really just sound the same (first time I'm saying this, I usually hate the 'it sounds the same' argument being said just because an album is sonically cohesive, because I love cohesive albums but these genuinely sound... kind of the same.) I feel that the amount of ballads and sad songs take away from the concept of trying to show the chapters of a relationship in different stages, because she was so into trying to incorporate the 'sad' aspect of a love song that she failed to capture the highs almost completely. I'm not saying the album is bad, i am saying the album is atleast mediocre and at most good but at the very baseline. I do genuinely believe 'the cure' is honestly one of the best songs released in recent times not just for Olivia. 'Drop dead' is pretty bland and I didn't get the praise when it was released, everyone acting as if it's an insane, extraordinary pop song. The part where I truly lose my braincells is the praising of 'maturity' on this album .. really? Maturity? There was a good deal of maturity in GUTS is what I will admit. I'm also a teenager but if anything it makes me more confused... did I really just see multiple people in their 30s, 40s, late 20s, whatever it is... say this album is mature? An album filled with angsty teenager songs like maggots for brains (shes a 'sad shell of a woman when her baby is away'), my way, ('here's the part where the girl gets pissed, the girls is me, you're in my way now'), drop dead (stalked you on the Internet, kiss me and I might drop dead, it's feminine intuition), expectations and a bunch of basic teen sadgirl ballads (not meant to be a bad thing but people calling it mature is getting to my brain) like purple, what's wrong with me (he's whats wrong with her, wow), less (very predictable meaning, wish he loved her less if it means he wont leave her), but no apparently the critics think it's mature too so im thinking bias? The thing is I'm not saying it has to be mature. I don't understand this entire 'maturity' discourse, let her write how she wants, she's like what.. 23? But the fact is it is NOT mature at all. One of the most teenager albums ever released. With GUTS I could understand. It has lacy, making the bed, the grudge, pretty isn't pretty and vampire which are well written and cover themes through a good lens. Even SOUR was more mature with jealousy jealousy, brutal and 1 step forward 3 steps back. I feel like everyone is using the cure as the single example for this album being 'mature.' I usually don't like cherrypicking lyrics from an album to act like the rest of the thing is bad but you can pick out a line that makes you go 'Oh wow...' from probably almost every song on the album. The aesthetics also feel very... it feels like she made a pinterest board and decided to run with that and force this new softer, feminine persona except it just doesn't feel natural. And please don't get me started with all the rock references. Atleast the cure is a good song but I get it, you're so very cultured. You like weezer, courtney love, the cure and you know all the drinking games. Has it ever been more common for singers to create an irritating personality along with their new music? They've always done it but it used to feel like the music was a reflection of THEIR current persona rather than THEM trying to be this forced version of the MUSIC instead. It's even crazier once you realise the album isn't even giving alcohol or rock girl anyway. Atleast she nailed the pinterest aesthetics?
label: criticism

text: Edit I did a pretty shit job of writing this yesterday, just read it back and realised just how much of it is horseshit. I'll leave it as it was originally written but with a clear head even I'll admit that a fair bit of what I wrote is just plain awful, apologies! Thanks for all the responses though, I just wanted to spark a discussion to find out a bit more about why he appeals to people. I know this isn't the most popular opinion to have (especially in this subreddit, I just searched Kanye and found so many people fanboy-ing over him), but I really can't see what the big fuss is all about with Kanye. Obviously, everyone is entitled to their own opinion on music and I would never criticise someone for liking/listening to his music but I really just don't see the appeal. When he was releasing songs like 'Gold Digger' and 'Touch The Sky' I was fairly interested, he was working with good music and some of his lyrics were pretty impressive. But since then I have constantly found him to be over-rated and I really don't see why people have such admiration for him. Lyrically, some of the stuff on Yeezus is just dreadful. Here's one of my favourite examples; Bound 2 I wanna fuck you hard on the sink After that, give you something to drink Step back, can't get spunk on the mink (This means nothing, if anything these lines continue the over-riding theme of the song that women are things which is great to have out for young people to see) I was going to do a couple more but it's so hard to pick individual lyrics out of the mounds of nothingness that he raps about. There are definitely some good ideas in Kanye's music, whether or not he has much to do with them is another matter. The man comes across as a complete tool as well, at first I assumed it was for publicity but he seems dumb on a George Bush Jr. kind of level. His Zane Lowe interview, the spat with Kimmel, and the various bits of footage of him being a dick to fans, I love this one in particular, make me have no interest in him. I'm sure many of you have arguments as to why Kanye is great in your eyes and I really look forward to reading them. Let battle commence.
label: criticism

text: I feel like it used to be easier to put artists into a specific category. Rock was rock, hip-hop was hip-hop, country was country. Now it seems like a lot of artists pull influences from everywhere. You can hear rap, pop, electronic, rock, R&B, and indie influences all in the same song. Part of me thinks that's one of the coolest things happening in music right now. Another part wonders if genres are starting to lose their meaning altogether. Do you think genres still matter as much as they used to, or are we moving toward a world where music is just music?
label: critique

text: We haven't had a major shake up in 25 years, something so profound that not only does it shake up the music industry, but it also changes culture. Yes, we've all heard the explanations before, lack of a mono culture, algorithms, the music industrys monopoly, no more gate-keepers, social media, technology, the overwhelming amount of music, etc, etc. But since any sort of anti-establishment, seismic shift has always been fueled by the 'younger generation'--I'm just not seeing any potential backlash/anger towards the mainstream with all this dopamine decadence. And it occurred to me...it might take a Gen X'er to spark a potential music revolution. There's an entire generation that only knows screen addiction, commercial music, influencers, content creation, etc. Hardly the danger we once had. That older age demographic has gone through it all, several music/cultural revolutions, turbulent times, they know what it's like to fight against the establishment, they lived through the music ideals like danger, authenticity, irreverence, controversey, recklessness, etc. My point is--since the younger generation seems to be apathetic towards actual change, it might be a bitter, resentful, angry older, band/artist to stir shit up again.
label: critique

text: I'll build my stance around the first two bands that come to my mind on the two ends of the spectrum: The Magnetic Fields and The Mars Volta. Stephin Merritt composes very simple melodies and writes really stripped down, to the point lyrics and yet the musical outcome is so emotional and more importantly it doesn't get old. The Magnetic Fields - All My Little Words It more and more seems to me like bands that choose the polar opposite approach to rock music, despite their intentions to be memorable and unique, fall short in timelessness. The Mars Volta - Roulette Dares These types of songs are no doubt very impressive and perhaps even more emotional than simple folk songs, but it's lack of an objective meaning, lack of compositional restraint, lack of minimalism just doesn't do it for me. It's like if I had to choose between an overcooked but ambitious bone-marrow coated poached duck liver over some pig-blood thai noodles and a perfectly fried chicken with corn salsa. Sure I'd go for the duck liver with amusement once in a while, but every other day I'd go for the chicken and lick that plate dry. Which could bring us to another point of the handful of bands that have achieved a perfect balance, like Pink Floyd - I mean, who doesn't like Pink Floyd? This may sound like an ignorant comment at first, but seriously, think about it, who doesn't like Pink Floyd? I think they are so great because they are incredible musicians who know every second of sound matters, who know less could be more, and more could also be more, both in moderation. I've been on a taste-in-music roller coaster all my adult life and this is where I'm at right now. Obviously I'm not arguing the moot point of 'which is better?'... I have my temporary point of view, you have yours - let's talk about it!
label: critique

text: If you were asked What band has had the most Top 40 singles in England how would you reply? The Beatles? No. The Rolling Stones? Nope. How about... Status Quo... the band whose lone US hit was the 1968 psychedelic smash Pictures Of Matchstick Men? According to Guinness World Records, Status Quo has amassed 68 separately recorded (credited) UK chart entries. (The Beatles have also had 68, but only 37 different ones; the remaining were reissues.) Of those 68 Status Quo singles, 22 reached the Top 10. The band's story began in 1962 when schoolmates Francis Rossi and Alan Lancaster formed a group called The Paladins, later renamed The Spectres. Four years later the quartet released three failed singles. Several changes happened in 1967. The band renamed themselves The Traffic Jam, released another unsuccessful single, added rhythm guitarist/vocalist Rick Parfitt, and became Status Quo. In January 1968, Status Quo released Pictures Of Matchstick Men. Its ringing guitar line and swirling phasing effects produced a psychedelic sound like nothing else on the radio. It rose to #7 in the UK and #12 on the US Hot 100. However, Status Quo was unable to maintain the momentum created by their hit in America. Pye Records dropped them and two years passed before they returned to the UK Top 10 with Paper Plane in 1973. During those lean years between Pictures Of Matchstick Men and Paper Plane, Status Quo with its simple riffs, catchy melodies, down-home lads image, and exciting live shows built a fiercely loyal fanbase that sustained them for decades. What fascinates me is how a band could become one of the biggest chart acts in British history while remaining largely unknown to many American listeners. For those familiar with Status Quo, why do you think their success never translated to the US on the same scale? And more broadly, what other artists do you think were massive in one country but never received the international recognition their success would suggest?
label: critique

text: Hardcore shows generally involve a lot of interaction between the band and the crowd and how the interaction is supposed to take place is something that's figured out through the course of the show which is a part of the fun. I just got back from a Maruja show, a four piece post-hardcore band from the UK (lead guitar/vocals, sax, bass and drums). It followed a lot of the expectations of a hardcore show with the band jumping into the crowd during heavier moments but there was also a type of performance art going on. In between songs or in quieter parts of songs the sax player or vocalist would move quietly into the crowd and stare at each member in the pit and there was clearly an unspoken rule being followed at that point from the band members: 'The boundaries between us might have been down a few moments ago. But now I am a performer. Not just in my space, but in your space.' The line is drawn and the performance art begins. Maruja are not the only post-hardcore band to be doing this. I read this in a live review of another post-hardcore band, Model/Actriz. 'While the crowd engulfed him in the mosh pit, the real highlights of the show came from the intense connection he had with the crowd, to a level I’ve never seen with any band before. At several moments, Cole would place himself head-to-head with an audience member, staring directly in their eyes and singing directly into their soul. It was so unique, haunting and uncomfortably intimate in a way that would not work with any other band.'And also Sprain, another post-hardcore band. At the 48:00-49:00 mark, the lead has a performative (I assume) emotional breakdown and for a moment there is an awkward silence where uncomfortable members of the audience laugh. The lead looks into the crowd and asks 'What's so fucking funny?'. This is just one example but their live performances seem to follow this trend. These three bands are making waves in the underground scene (although Sprain recently broke up). Is there more of a taste now for this sort of performance and if so, why? Are there other performers outside of the hardcore/post-hardcore scene that are both being widely recognised and integrating performance art in this manner into their live shows? Or have these sorts of bands always been trending to some degree and I'm just unaware? Look forward to hearing people's thoughts
label: critique

text: I have recently become intrigued by this topic. As a long time k-pop fan it was very typical for a band to have its members start solo careers, but typically towards the end of the life of the band (about 5 or 7 years, for luckier bands 10 years after debut). It was always an interesting process to see how the individual members would express themselves but it also could be a dumpster fire, especially for the group/band as whole and their future endeavors. This concept only returned to my mind after recently getting into Maneskin and subsequently learning that they are on a break...to pursue solo interests. It's interesting isn't it? A break/hiatus, solo interests. In the west, a hiatus (one that is made official not just the band being quiet for a while) and the solo endeavors for a band typically spells out disaster for the band itself to put it lightly. Just look at 1 Direction, 5th Harmony and the endless list, but those weren't Italian rock bands but they do have something in common, being made famous by xfactor/music competition shows . In k-pop it is typically less tragic because it typically only occurs when the group has already or has started to fade out not at there peak or right after it (exceptions do exist), but here we have group in their prime taking a break. To me it usually means the following: a. One member or more feels (and or management thinks) that they are fairly popular enough to pull off a 'Beyoncé' (become successful beyond the group on there own). b. there is some element of internal conflict making it hard for them to work together/priorities have shifted requiring them to take a break and maybe find something else to do in the meantime which will end up being solo work to help maintain relevance and or find themselves musically. c. problems with whoever helps them release their music (e.g. record label) making it hard to do things together but maybe not necessarily on their won. d. they are genuinely just taking a break for whatever reason but not due to internal conflict or a Beyoncé situation. Regardless of the reason (but especially if it is a or b), this typically has a negative impact on the group and its music. The reunion is rarely as electric or to put it lightly like what the band was like before. Even in k-pop where it is basically scheduled ahead of the time for the lowest risk period of a band's lifespan, it typically spells itself out the same. When they do comeback, it is often what I have come to call 'reunion music', something made purely just to keep the fans at bay, maybe the contracual obligation comes to mind but in my opinion most bands do participate willingly and enthusiastically. It often feels like a watered down version of themselves with some nostalgia bait thrown in either on purpose or by default because maybe they had a certain music style that brings back the memories etc. This effect isn't really felt if the band remains active during the 'solos' and still releases music as they release solo work on their usual schedule. I also find that if they seemingly take a hiatus without actually having to announce it, it is not as severe, perhaps because things weren't so bad that they had to announce a hiatus. While I do think it a basic right for a member of a band to have a solo career, I also think that it is hard to balance both a band obligation and a solo career. I think part of the reason the reunion release tends to suck is because priorities and interests of the band members have shifted. It is not rare that a member's own work will be wildly different from the main band's work and trying to apply that back to the band can be difficult. I also think if they don't have a clear vision for the band, then things will naturally fall apart when they return. Perhaps as a member does solo work they suddenly realise they don't want to do what they band used to do anymore as that's not how they want to express themselves in the moment. I find that for bands (k-pop) with both successful soloists and band careers, there is a certain level of compromise/sacrifice happening on both ends. A soloist in a band has to manage both the creative work required for the band and their solo careers like 2 different projects at the same time which obviously limits the commitment they can put into their own work which often seems to limit their solo success. Some examples I would give would be Girl's Generations (GG/SNSD) Taeyeon when she intially first went solo. She was huge like mega huge but she seemed to tone it down a little (or her management did) to not overshadow GG all together since they still had plans together, Taemin from Shinee comes to mind too. If they wanted they could have easily just left their bands behind. A funny example of a member being simultaneously famous as a soloist and in big band was Gdragon (a literal icon of the industry) when he was in BigBang and I have no idea how they made that work but they did operate simultaneously. He was bigger than the band but the band still remained popular, infact more popular than other bands operating at the time. There are also plenty of examples of this going wrong with members of certain bands leaving or being heavily prioritised because their solo careers took off and a ghost band being left behind. Enough of the k-pop, I am just curious if you think it is a realistic dynamic for a band to hand both responsibilities? I know it doesn't have to be but it feels like by the time a group is going solo, it means the band is about to die. That's why I am kind of concerned abour Maneskin's situation with fans getting excited to even see the members in the same place because it seems like a rare occurrence now. I am bummed out because I liked how audacious they were and they kind of gave me hope (for some reason, it's not like they make uplifiting music) perhaps maybe that's what the (my) world needs right now. Of course they have no obligation to serve my interests, I am jusy curious as to if my brain is making this pattern up.
label: critique

text: (Oh boy I’m going to get downvoted for this I can already tell) There’s actually one specific problem I have with it that I’ve never seen anyone bring up, but I think is a pretty significant flaw. Where are the band members besides Brian? Occasionally there’ll be like 1 or 2 other members and for the most part that’s it. It kind of makes me wonder what the point of bringing all these session musicians in if you’re not going to use your actual band mates that much. Obviously the songs themselves are great, I’m not going to pretend that this ruins the album for me and that it’s now a 0/10, it’s just that I’m not sure why this is a beach boys album and not a Brian Wilson solo project. Obviously though, the album itself is astronomically exceptional. I would just love to hear other people’s thoughts on this because it does genuinely bug me a little bit.
label: criticism

text: I started with Kate on The Sensual World (Love and Anger got me) and rapidly picked up Hounds of Love (title track hooked me) and have been branching out with her ever since. Wuthering Heights is an odd little song, but somehow very catchy to me, and I have come to love The Dreaming as the strangest masterpiece in my collection. I've heard folks say they don't 'get' Kate Bush or have a hard time with her music and am curious to see what they bounce off of. To me, Sensual World is the easiest entry point with Love and Anger and This Woman's Work being the easiest entry points. Is the music just too strange? Is it the female perspective? Her odd vocals? I know it's probably an acquired taste, but for whatever reason, I have never had trouble with Kate's oddness, for me it's more a charm. Curious to know
label: appreciation

text: It is been almost a year since 'Dont tap the glass' by Tyler, the creator dropped and for some good measure I want to discuss how you guys feel about this album and what you think are some of its high or low lights and why you think it is like this. For start the album opens up with an voice explaining the rules of DTTG with a dancy funky beat immediately after and big energy, I think 'Big Poe' is one of the highlights of this album as it is high energy with a beat that made me dance at 11 in the morning when it first dropped. While in the middle of the album are some more popular songs like 'Sugar on my tongue' I think the Peak of the album is rather near the end with the deluxe track 'Down Bad' and 'Tell me what it is' it is such a beautiful ending for such a good album and overall I think that this is a good project even tho it still has some flaws, as it is to unstructured for my personal opinion. Overall it is a good album and I actually like it and have some of its songs on my personal repeat. Now what is your opinion, would you agree or do you disagree with this take?
label: appreciation

text: So in the past week, I've seen a trend where people say 'maturing is realizing that Lauryn Hills music is about God' and I got confused because that was not how I interpreted it. This is not fact just my opinion and I would like to hear yours,do you agree or I'm an idiot? The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill is one of the best albums ever created, not just by a female artist, but of all time. Yet there is still a lot of misinterpretation of this work. This album is not about God. Lauryn Hill uses God as a moral judge of problems on Earth, she calls on God for help and repentance. She is not talking about God, she turns to God seeking help. 'Tough Love' is the main topic of this album. The idea, prevalent in Black communities, that a child's first bully is their parents, who harden them to prepare them for the racism out in the world. This is the type of love Lauryn has been gaslit into believing is true love. In the process of unlearning this, Lauryn Hill realizes that this love is a result of fear. Fear of all the things out in the world, fear passed down through generations, generational trauma. Perpetrators usually think they are protecting their children yet they end up becoming the exact monsters they fear. Song Analyses Ex-Factor: The Anatomy of Abuse 'Ex-Factor' is the richest song on the album in terms of depth. It talks about Lauryn's experience in an abusive relationship where her partner resorts to self-harm and manipulation to keep her. Lauryn admits to wanting the relationship to continue, that she loves her partner, but understands that this cannot carry on. She cannot keep being lied to, exploited and abused, having the very values she was taught love was completely undermined. Doo Wop (That Thing): A Warning 'Doo Wop (That Thing)' is Lauryn warning the youth about gold digging women and male gangsters, liars and manipulators. She is telling the youth to respect themselves and their significant others because this kind of love will cost them. When It Hurts So Bad: The Sequel 'When It Hurts So Bad' is kind of a sequel to 'Ex-Factor.' A continuation of Lauryn trying her hardest to keep the relationship going but just keeps hurting herself in the process, while simultaneously moving on to better men yet still chasing the feeling she is infatuated with. What she wants is hurting her while she overlooks the type of love she actually deserves. Forgive Them Father: The Turning Point 'Forgive Them Father' is the most important song on the album. Lauryn turns to God praying for forgiveness on behalf of her abusers, manipulators and wolves in sheep's clothing. She questions and confronts the men who hurt her yet still prays for them, claiming they do not know what they are doing, that they are just perpetuating the hate they received themselves, guised in love and guidance. This is Lauryn letting go and accepting that 'everything is everything,' whatever happened to her was meant to happen for her to grow. She learns to forgive and move on. Nothing Even Matters: The Arrival 'Nothing Even Matters' is Lauryn Hill maturing and discovering what love truly is. Love is a harmless drug and 'what if I go through withdrawals.' She does not care what people think anymore because nothing even matters except love. She does not need to dress up or do her hair for love. This song embodies Black, gentle coconut oil love. After everything she has been through, she now knows what love is and nothing else matters.
label: appreciation

text: Always been a fan of the band, but for the past month-ish or so, I’ve really enjoyed their early era - particularly their 1978 debut album “The Scream”. There are so many elements that I love about that part of the band’s career. I’ll start with John McKay (who was the guitarist for the Banshees from 1977 to 1979). I think he’s an incredible and hugely underrated guitarist…..I’m not a musician, nor do I know much about music theory, so I’m simply going by ear here. But to me, the things that he did on the guitar were incredible. I’m impressed with the uniqueness of his style….I got this imagery of spikes & barbed wire whenever I’m focusing on his work. John McKay’s guitar parts had this angular & jagged element…..like you’re getting chopped up by a really sharp blade. And when the Banshees covered the Beatles’ “Helter Skelter”….OMG! The intro literally sounds like a precursor to Sonic Youth or even Big Black. I think that unusual chords were involved too….the stuff that John McKay did can’t be replicated by anybody in my opinion. And I dig the more uncompromising mood. I feel that something like Juju is more colorful & psychedelic, whereas The Scream has more of an edge to it. You can really hear the punk background of the Banshees….the riffing & the faster parts are very similar to the Sex Pistols & the Buzzcocks. And The Scream has this harshness & a tougher, more stark feel that I really like….things like the dissonance of “Pure”, the last minute or so of “Suburban Relapse”….it felt so raw to me. Felt like I was in a horror film! I dig other elements of the Banshees’ early days too, like the drumming that reminded me of CAN & Moe Tucker’s work for the Velvet Underground. And the supple basslines & Siouxsie Sioux’s Bowie-ish vocals. I like to compare The Scream with other post-punk groups like Wire, Magazine & Gang of Four. Great stuff overall!
label: appreciation

text: Insane Clown Posse might be the most hated music group in modern history. More than Nickelback, more than Imagine Dragons, more than Limp Bizkit, more than any of them. Unlike those 3, ICP NEVER had support from media, and their fans were designated a gang. But even though musically the group is certainly not everyone's cup of tea, they are honestly a lot better than people give them credit for. They get judged harshly because they're not 'serious' rappers- what the hell do you expect from guys who dress up in clown makeup and spray Faygo over their audience? Illmatic Part 2? But they are great storytellers and have a solid sense of humor. Some songs I recommend are: '12' from Riddle Box. This song is a great concept song. It’s about a jury of 12 and a guy who was sentenced to death by them for a crime he didn’t commit. So he comes back to kill all 12 that condemned him. 'The Pendulum's Promise' from Bizaar. This song is about people judging others, and don't realize... they are also in many ways describing themselves. 'Crossing Thy Bridge' from The Wraith: Shangri La. This is borderline metal. It's about being horrified at seeing children die at a young age, and talking to God about wanting redemption. 'Halls Of Illusions' from The Great Milenko. Probably one of the closest things these guys have to a 'hit'. A great concept song about taking child abusers and woman beaters on a deadly ride representing a fun-house to reflect upon their actions, as they are shown the consequences of their ways as they pass through the 'Halls of Illusions.' If you want to check out those songs, do so and give ICP a chance. If you just blindly go by the 'ICP sucks' mentality that you're supposed to think because Eminem famously dissed them and Miracles went viral... more power to you. You're not gonna want to drink Faygo and paint your face up after listening to these tracks, but it could give you a solid look at the other side of the clowns.
label: appreciation

text: Since discovering Jimmy Cauty’s Space, I’ve become fascinated by the incredibly brief creative partnership between Cauty and Alex Paterson. It feels as though something special happened whenever these two crossed paths. They were the original incarnation of The Orb. Together they developed those early ambient DJ sets, worked on 'A Huge Ever Growing Pulsating Brain That Rules from the Centre of the Ultraworld,' and began the project that eventually became Space before their partnership fell apart. Then they separated, and this extraordinary cluster of music emerged around them: Space, Chill Out and The Orb’s Adventures Beyond the Ultraworld. Obviously, the precise credits and chronology are complicated. Paterson has long claimed that he contributed significantly to Chill Out (on one podcast he claimed it was 80% him!), while Space was reportedly stripped of his contributions and reworked by Cauty following their split. There are competing accounts, bruised feelings and probably no completely objective version of what happened. But I’m less interested in figuring out who made what percentage of each record than I am in the creative relationship itself. Listening to the original 'Pulsating Brain,' Space, Chill Out and the early Orb material, I keep wondering whether Cauty and Paterson brought something unique out of each other. Perhaps Paterson supplied the endless curiosity, samples, strange juxtapositions and free-associative flow, while Cauty brought a particular sense of shape, atmosphere and conceptual scale. That is just interpretation, but together they seemed capable of making music that felt enormous, playful, mysterious and completely outside ordinary time. Both men made brilliant work without each other, so this isn’t about diminishing either of them. But that short overlap feels almost alchemical. It helped establish a musical language that both The Orb and the KLF would then carry in different directions. Had they stayed together longer, would The Orb have become what it became? Would Chill Out exist in the same form? Would Space now be regarded as an ambient classic rather than an obscure KLF-adjacent curiosity? Or was the brevity and tension of their partnership exactly why that music was so special? I’d love to hear from people who know this period better. What do you think each of them brought to the partnership, and where can you hear it most clearly?
label: appreciation

text: First time writing my thoughts on a album.All Things Must Pass takes you on the journey of a child who is growing up and learning to know himself.The beauty of the album comes from the movement between George trying to understand himself and moments of acceptance and growth. You experience that journey alongside him, sharing the subtle feelings of being ungrounded, uncertain, and quietly searching for meaning. You can hear this most clearly in Isn’t It a Pity, where the song feels as though it is searching for an understanding rather than building toward an event.What carries the song isn’t the expectation of a climax, but the desire to understand what George himself is trying to understand.He answers that question with patience and observation instead of dramatic resolution.The soul of the album comes from a place of acceptance, and perhaps even transcendence. George’s courage to look at life as it is, while choosing to embrace its brighter side, becomes the album’s central message. You can feel this in If Not for You, where he simply takes us somewhere beautiful and lets us stay there. The melody has a brightness that feels as though it is painting the world around it, inviting us to experience that warmth with him.As George moves from seeing the world with the openness of a child to facing and accepting the realities of adulthood, he never loses his gentleness. Behind That Locked Door captures this beautifully. It accepts that the world can be painful, yet still worth living in. Rather than resisting that truth, George embraces it with compassion.Phil Spector’s dense production gives the album a mystical, almost fantasy-like atmosphere that can make these experiences feel distant or out of reach. Yet George’s songwriting always brings them back down to earth. Beneath the grand production is someone sharing deeply human experiences, reminding us that this journey is not unique to him. It is something anyone can experience if they are willing to open their heart.
label: appreciation

text: We've had some threads about how different types of websites, forums, magazines, and music critic fanbases will have tastes that lean towards certain directions, artists, and albums. I've read discussions about mu-core, rym-core, fantano-core, topster, the general ratings on 1001 albums, best-ever albums, etc. Plus magazines like Rolling Stone, Pitchfork, NME, SPIN, etc. For several of these, there's a general sense of people leaning towards indie, alternative, and experimental works alongside popular canonical works. But one category I don't quite understand is 'Reddit Music Taste'. I would see commenters talking about how a certain band or artist is really beloved by Reddit like Queen or David Bowie. Since they're just popular artists in general, it doesn't really tell me much. My best guess on what reddit music taste might entail is a sort of strong memetic admiration for an artist. It's usually artists that strike a critical acclaim and commercial success sweet spot. You might be able to predict what comment is going to be upvoted right to the top.
label: critique

text: It really bothers me when songs sound similar, even if its a superficial resemblance. Recently my response has been to start making mashups. My first is David Bowie's "Fame" with Parliament's "Give up the Funk". I heard Parliament's first via MC Hammer's sample on his first album and I assumed Bowie was copying Parliament. Turns out it was the other way around. Great songs, it's just funny how well they fit when layered on top of each other

text: You're making a very weak argument when it comes to context. You're assuming my headspace is stuck on a poppy or accessible by default, which has nothing to do with context. It's implied you should know there different types of music and sounds.If I were listening to a black metal record, I'd know what to expect, not because of the context behind the album, but from experience and knowledge; I've heard the genre before, I've read about it.Influences are irrelevant. Musicians couldn't care less about who they remind someone of unless they are on a tribute band. I've played and written music before, I have several friends who are decently big on my local scene; they all hate being asked about influences and inspirations.Lyrics are extremely subjective. Besides, you do not need context to decipher a piece of poetry or writing; that's why you are usually given stray poems in English exams for you to analyze at face value.I could like a phrase or metaphor in a song, which doesn't make too much sense, being abstract and what not. I could also dislike a line which has some context and references a different topic.Context could make me understand lyrics, but that doesn't necessarily mean I'll appreciate them
label: criticism

text: Let's look at the classic rock band KISS. They have switched genres quite a bit, starting with standard rock type stuff, then moving on in the late 70s to disco, then to power pop, then to a progressive rock concept album. Then, off came the makeup and they hired guitarist Vinnie Vincent, a known shredder, to update their sound for the 80s rock scene. After that, they did hair metal, returned to a harder, more metal sound in the 90s, and finally did an album of Alice in Chains style grunge. Then, they put the makeup back on and started retreading old ground. Next, let's look at one of the all-time great singers of the rock era, Michael Bolton. He started out singing bluesy rock music in the mid-70s, was the lead singer for a Journey-esque AOR band in the late 70s/early 80s, switched to synth and gated reverb-heavy hair metal in the mid-80s, and then, in the late 80s and early 90s, the soulful ballads he is most famous for. By the late 90s, he was doing then-contemporary RnB music, and then in the early 2000s, adult contemporary with a pop flavor (this song wouldn't sound out of place on a Josh Groban record). 2009 style change, with Bolton working with hot contemporary producers to blend the contemporary pop style with his unique soulfulness. His most recent album, Spark of Light, is pretty much in the same vein, but sounds more timeless. To say nothing of his many cover albums, of which Vintage is probably the best (but they're all great). Neither KISS nor Bolton got much artistic or critical respect for all those genre changes, but meanwhile, Taylor Swift, let's actually look at Tay Tay for a minute. She started off doing country-flavored pop (her music was never truly country), transitioned into doing pure pop, and then, in 2020, she released two 'indie folk' albums, which actually were indie/folk flavored pop, and critics went crazy for them, and articles were constantly being written about how great and artistic she was. Also, let's keep in mind here that even her early 'country' stuff doesn't sound that different from her later pop and 'indie/folk' stuff, which makes sense, since, like I said, she's not doing actual country or indie/folk, just pop music flavored with those genres. 'Detroit Rock City' sounds nothing like 'Tomorrow' sounds nothing like 'My Way' sounds nothing like 'It Never Goes Away'. 'Every Day of My Life' sounds nothing like 'Maybe It's the Power of Love' sounds nothing like 'Paradise' sounds nothing like 'Start Breaking My Heart' sounds nothing like 'Now That I Found You' sounds nothing like 'Why Me' sounds nothing like 'Love With My Eyes Closed' sounds nothing like 'Survivor'. Yes, I understand that KISS and Bolton both was mostly chasing trends and not creating them, but so was Swift. Once her career blew up in the aftermath of Ye interrupting her, she switched over to an even more commercial style than country-pop and started working with the biggest producers (i.e. Max Martin, Shellback), and then, as indie/folk stuff became more popular throughout the 2010s, she capitalized on the trend in 2020. She is no less of a trend-chaser than KISS and Bolton, at least Bolton and Paul Stanley and Gene Simmons (the main singers for KISS) are/were actually good vocalists (Stanley sounds pretty brutal these days, but he was an amazing singer back in the 80s and 90s).
label: critique

text: you pretty much answered your own question, though I'd add that Kiss and Bolton were never especially popular among critics anyway for a variety of reasons.Taylor going from Country Pop to "Pure Pop" seems like a lateral moves, and you pretty much say as much on her Indie Folk turn. Whereas Kiss chasing down every single emerging trend as it pops up seems much more brazen in its attempts to stay relevant (not to mention that Kiss often didn't do these things especially well or without controversy among their own fanbase; i.e., I Was Made For Loving You). The former is part of a natural evolution to a degree while the latter is a bit offputting and reeks of opportunism.That said, I'm also not sure Taylor is really getting praised solely for switching sounds, other than maybe the switch from country pop to pop means she leaves the baggage of the former behind.
label: criticism

text: I’d disagree with the contention that Kiss didn’t chase trends well, apart from maybe their attempt at a concept album. They were generally pretty consistent at coming up with concise, poppy, hook-oriented songs rooted in British glam. A lot of classic rock acts tried to do a disco song - Rolling Stones, the Kinks, the Who, Pink Floyd, Rod Stewart, Grateful Dead, etc, plus a lot of new wavers and everyone who brought in Nile Rodgers when they ran out of ideas in the 80s - I think Kiss did disco pretty well and personally I find the disco flair and production added to a lot of the spruced up tracks on Double Platinum serves them well. The question of the respectability of Kiss or the influence of disco on rock or genre-surfing might be interesting if it weren’t framed through comparisons with Taylor Swift
label: appreciation

text: So here lately I've been really listening to a lot of John Denver as I find he provides me lots of catharsis in the stressful times we are now living in. My love for his music can be traced back three generations now, It started with my Grandma who would play John Denver with my dad and his brothers when they would take trips half way across the nation to California back in the late 1970's / early 1980's and often times when passing through the Redwood National forest. This made an impact on my dad who many years later took me with him at a young age to get the live album known as 'An Evening with John Denver'. We listened to it all the way home and I it was so different, John Denver's lyrics of love, nature and joy of life itself were very comforting, almost like being sung a lullaby, as was the gentle strum of his guitar, I was entranced by the majestic Rocky Mountain High. These days, I struggle with lots of the same things many of you do. Career concerns, fear of loss, occasional confusion and upset with the current state of the world, but then I listen to John Denver and he tells me don't worry about any of that, look at the mountains and the streams, you see all these living trees that make oxygen for you to breath, don't you just fucking love that! Hell yes I do! as far as I am concerned his music is what a Bob Ross painting would sound like. Thanks for the music John, I am going to plant a tree in your name. Wish you were here so I could tell you how much your work is helping me deal with a tough time in my own life. Your end was tragic but your music has rippled through my family for three generations now and will for many more. You made a lasting impact on me and my family. Thanks for reading.
label: appreciation

text: The best show I've ever seen was D'Angelo and The Vanguard on a pretty big stage at a festival but I've also seen some great shows from smaller artists on small stages. I've seen some artists blow up and put on good shows from tiny stages to arenas. It really just depends how much effort the artist is willing to put into it. A lot of smaller artists are working really hard to establish themselves and then they either keep that attitude or drop it when they feel they've made it. I don't think rap artists are typically very good live and I don't think that is limited to bigger artists. I've seen awful and great performances from both local MCs and bigger artists. S. Reidy out of Oklahoma absolutely kills it live because he's passionate about what he's doing. In contrast, a lot of bigger artists (for example: Lil Wayne is notoriously bad live) are basically just doing the ad-libs and every tenth word of the verses. However, there's some successful artists like Freddie Gibbs, Denzel Curry, Vince Staples, Kendrick, etc. that still put in the effort and have great live shows a decade or more into their careers.
label: critique

text: I'd already heard Another Brick part 2 and Comfortably Numb from pop culture, needless to say obvious masterpieces, but everything else was new to me. I actually think Mother and Outside The Wall (especially the movie version for the latter) are two of my favourite songs now. I'm kind of obsessed with Mother lyrically, I feel like such intelligent depictions of parents who fuck their kids up are rare (coming from someone with not great parents, lol) - the way her darker, controlling side is sort of masked underneath a surface level vibe of caring and love is so effective and creepy, and very true to life as well (everyone is the hero of their own story after all, she thinks she's doing a fine job raising her son). I think my favourite examples of this are 'she won't let you fly, but she might let you sing/mama's gunna keep baby cosy and warm' and 'you'll always be baby to me' - squint and don't think too hard and you'll be like 'gaww' but look twice and the darkness of it is so evident, honestly hits like a truck. As for Outside The Wall... man, not only is it a perfect and poignant summary of all the themes, but the lyricism again! 'Some stagger and fall, after all it's not easy, banging your heart against some mad bugger's wall'... we've got a total lack of blame on the people who have to walk away from Pink and people like him for their own safety, and 'mad bugger' both places the responsibility where it ultimately belongs (on Pink) but has such an endearing and sympathetic quality to it at the same time (I kept mis-singing it as 'poor bugger' because that's how it feels). And 'banging your heart' is one of those lines which just makes perfect sense emotionally, obviously evoking an idiom but twisting it to emphasise the emotional strain of trying to connect to people behind walls, I love how poetic it is. I also love the contrast between the album and film versions - album has a much more melancholy tone to it, and there's the famous 'isn't this where?' thing, which makes it feel really hopeless - yeah, he's outside his wall now, but that means he's vulnerable to getting hurt and so this cycle will just keep going. But the film has a way more triumphant tone, it's more celebratory of his escape (the uplifting horns and the singing vs speaking of the lyrics especially help with this) and there's no 'isn't this where?' implying he is just free now, and can start to properly rebuild his life. I feel like both are really effective in different ways, the album is maybe a better cautionary tale for people not yet behind walls, but the film offers hope to those already in the process of building theirs that escape is possible and worth trying if that makes sense. I love both but the film version is so gorgeous to me... the strings, the horns, the humming of the choir, it's absolutely beautiful sonically.
label: appreciation

text: He’s basically a child prodigy…joined the RHCP in his late teens and all of those experiences has lead him a life to spend a lot of his time absorbing, considering and creating music. Some in that position really don’t take it that deep and yeah he got sidetracked with drugs for a lot of years but even in his addiction he seemed to be writing and playing music.Although I don’t know him I met him several times when he was doing his synth projects, going to underground synth events and I must say he was always very nice and even a little bit quiet…he absolutely knew the cool synth music from Avant Garde electronic composers from the 60’s & 70’s as well as some of the cool break core, IDM folks much more unknown than Aphex Twin & Squarepusher.Again, I don’t really know him but when he was around he was super nice and open to talk to folks…seemed a lot cooler than I might of thought of him in the past…might have been the years of drug addiction or maybe a little ASD(aspy as they say)…but one thing that was clear to me is that his influences were wide and deep.

text: I'm not saying he isn't, but many are way overstating just how great- probably mostly non-musicians or novice players- and also more than likely conflating his writing abilities with his technical prowess. His writing skills are absolutely undeniable and proven to be world-class, but that's different to his playing- I'll guarantee in your town, assuming it's at least a small-mid-sized one, there's several players who could, from a technical perspective, way outshine him. I don't know why it's so hard for people to grasp that... I'm a big fan of the guys music, but also am not letting that cloud my objective judgement. It just is what it is. He's a fairly ordinary player with exceptional writing abilities which made some of the biggest hits of the past nearly 40 years.

text: He’s my favorite artist of all time, I think he knows how to craft an emotional song or melody in a way that resonates with me better than most musicians. text: I honestly don't understand why "complexity" is equated with merit in guitar communities. There is nothing technically deficient about Frusciante. He is an extremely technical guitarist, and his obsessive attention to detail is evident across every dimension of his production and playing.His guitar tone always leaps out at you. Every note in his solos feels carefully chosen as a result of iteration and the slow cultivation of happy accidents. He chooses to show off not with speed or complexity but by leaning into what is called "sloppiness" and into groove and savouring every pitch bend and knowing when not to add a single thing every time a chord rings simply like a bell in the depths of noon. He can play a single sweet little sustained note on the fretboard and imbue that unadorned note somehow with sorrow and joy. What you refer to as innocence is sheer delight in and childlike play with the voice of a guitar. A return to the fundamentals. His gift is in knowing when not to play just as much as when to play, and for every sound he makes to be part of his range of expression
label: appreciation

text: A few months ago, I was in the car with my father, and he had his YouTube Music mix on, and he listens to a lot of country. One song that came up, that neither he nor I had ever heard, was 'Dinosaur' by Hank Williams Jr. This song appears to be about styles of music that Hank Jr dislikes. This song is not very good. When listening to this song, I was reminded of a song I have, for a long time, not cared for: 'Old Time Rock and Roll' by Bob Seger. I don't really like that song because it's mostly about how he doesn't like current (at the time) styles of music, and that always rubbed me the wrong way, and when I heard 'Dinosaur,' I finally put my finger on why. Music is about sharing thoughts and experiences, and wholesale writing off entire genres is something of an inherently anti-musical idea, which does not translate to song. Other examples include whenever Arrested Development or Common would put down gangsta rappers, the parts of 'Pop Muzik' by M where he says he doesn't like rock, and 'Death to Mumble Rap'/any number of adjacent anti-Soundcloud rap songs by tryhard Eminem wannabes.
label: criticism

text: Many people fix their music tastes during their teenage years, and even though I'm an exception to that, I still do like a lot of music from that time (early 80s). However, I wasn't a big fan of the popular music I heard before I was a teenager (70s). I liked a bit of it, but I considered most of it to be garbage. Maybe you feel that way about the music you grew up with as a teenager. However, I did later discover music from the 70s that I did like a lot. It's just that really bad music was getting more airplay at that time, and I wasn't exposed to it until later. As you explore music more, you should find music you enjoy from earlier time periods. After all, the music in each time period is diverse, and even if you haven't liked what you've heard from a particular decade or generation, that doesn't mean you won't find something you do like from back then.Since you mentioned classical as one of the genres you like, I'll point out that most classical music is older than ten years old. You should check out Bach, Beethoven, Mozart,Tchaikovsky, Debussy, Ravel, Holst, Stravinsky, and Copland, among others. They all lived before you were born but did some great music. Jazz is another genre that was well established before you were born. It's worth checking out Raymond Scott, Duke Ellington, Miles Davis, Dave Brubeck, the Modern Jazz Quartet, Bob James, Chick Corea, Keiko Matsui, and Hiromi, among others. Some of the latter ones are still active, but they all have some albums older than ten years old. What classical and jazz are you listening to that is no older than ten years old?
label: critique

text: Complaining is generally the cornerstone of debating about any music, isn’t it?What constitutes “Good” music is inherently subjective, and disagreement often takes the form of complaining about others’ taste, or the dominant perceived views in a scene/genre/era whatever.The “elitist rap bias” I got from the post is more like domination of a few big name artists that prevent people from diving deeper into the music, including the past. Rather than obsession over “the elite” or “best” artists from a critical/high art perspective.My take on this, is that most people in general are simply casual music listeners, while OP is apparently more serious/thoughtful about it than their peers. I experienced this as well frowning up, but not with rap so much, as that wasn’t my primary genre.OP you may need to accept that most people aren’t going to be interested in diving deeper into debates over production techniques or lyrical influences, and just want to listen to whatever is currently cool or use music as background noise. But fellow aficionados are out there, and it is rewarding when you meet them. Maybe check out some music shops, record stores, bars/clubs in your area where musicians are more likely to hang out?Or maybe I totally misinterpreted the assignment. I do that sometimes on here.
label: critique

text: I would encourage anyone who thinks they 'don’t like country' to listen to the old school 70’s country first. Artists like Waylon Jennings, old school Willie Nelson, then hit some more Americana artists like Jason Isbell, Sturgill Simpson. Most country you find on the radio or CMT is gonna be pop with twangy guitars and formulaic lyrics. Real country music is true American roots music and it good shit. It’s similar to hip hop in that it is music that defines a certain slice of the American working class rural experience vs the inner city African American experience w hip hop
label: appreciation

text: I feel like it would take a huge cultural moment to change those opinions on what country music is. Heck, people like Zach Bryan and Chris Stapleton might have a hand in doing that.I held a lot of those opinions for a long time- my experience growing up was that country music was the soundtrack to the Bush administration, 9/11 fearmongering, and all the guys who called me homophobic slurs in high school. I think that sucks, because I've since discovered so much great country music. The country scene where I live is actually really similar to the punk scene and now I've been able to see that- just like basically any other genre- that huge, radio version of it is just the top 1% most commercial stuff, and it sucks compared to people making music in 80-capacity bars.If someone wants to avoid a whole genre of music because that's their perception, that's fine I guess, the world isn't really missing out on anything if they do. I just always try to introduce them to the vehemently socialist, gay, indigenous, anarchist country music that's out there.Also, since you mentioned Big Thief, I'm trying so hard to get "post-country" to happen. It works for Big Thief, Wednesday, MJ Lenderman.
label: appreciation

text: This is such a good topic. I, a 40-something Midwestern white dude, dislike both hip-hop and country for the same reason: It does not speak to me or my experiences. I think the biggest difference is modern country comes with a not-so-subtle moral superiority complex. If your view of hip-hop is that it’s just a bunch of thugs talking about doing thug shit, yeah, that’s racist as fuck and you should be called out on it.Having good flow, being able to produce good beats, those things take immense talent. I had the pleasure of being in a band with a former Nashville studio artist, a pedal steel player, and he made me weep with his talent.But the “our way of life is better than those city folks” message of modern country is so off-putting that people hate it for what it is.
label: criticism

text: Long rambling thoughts ahead. Seeing multiple threads on the subject got me thinking, but it's the holidays and I'm a few in, so pardon my syntax.regarding the biases people have with country music and the culture that can surround it, should these ideas be challenged or even dismantled?I'd be interested in data. How many country radio listeners reliably vote far right. How does that compare to alt-country listeners. etc. What's the demographics etc etc etc. If people's views are based on inaccuracies, we need to at least get a better understanding of the listenership to reorient our understanding. FWIW, my differences with rural North Americans come out of deeper tensions between urbanites and their provincial counterparts, I think musical differences are symptom, not a cause.Is it necessarily a good or acceptable thing that many people perceive all country as right-wing/bigoted material, or not? For people who have these particular negative ideas instilled, those who avoid all of country for either of the reasons above (or any other reasons outside of not enjoying country's sound, although going in-depth as to why no artists from the genre work for you is very much still a point of interest) is there any reason why you haven't budged from your position yet? Would you ever consider doing so?I should state that country isn't something I really hate. I don't listen to it at all, and no one around me does, but it doesn't harm me and I have have no real beef with anyone who enjoys it. If anything, I've historically been far more annoyed with Indie - the music of the bourgeois hipsters I've been surrounded by as a city-dweller. Country feels more distant than K-Pop nowadays.But since you asked why I'm musically uninterested in country, it's because the ideas that most interest me are rhythm and futurism (in the sci-fi sense). It would be ridiculous of me to ask country, as a genre, to become drum & bass or Hip Hop rhythmically - that's not what it's audience wants and when the genre attempts that (bro country) we hate it anyways cause it sounds janky as hell. If anything country - from what I can tell - works best when it's steeped in traditionalism and melody.I just don't see a way to square the circle of country as pleasing its audience and pleasing me, someone in a totally different life situation. I've lived in cities my whole life and been surrounded by music that reflects my environment for the lion's share of my life so far. I suspect the rap and country duality you've brought up is a meaningful one because these are musics that aspire to reflect their audiences (while also being money making enterprises that aren't above selling cartoonishly exaggerated personas, there's a healthy amount of kayfabe in both).At the end of the day, not everyone's going to like everything and I believe that ultimately, music is a social experience - it says who we are to our peers and it becomes a communal experience shared with them.,but it's also a window into other people, and if you give music a chance, it can break down barriers.I'm a huge fan of Jamaican music, particularly the rhythms and sonics of late 90s early 00s dancehal, but really everything from Ska on has been incredibly boundary pushing, futuristic music and it's had a massive worldwide impact. Now, I'm not Jamaican, and beyond going to dancehall events, I wanted to know more about the genre, and what do you know: Jamaican music has a longstanding love affair with country. Way back in the 60s-70s/20th century, they'd get US radio stations and, being a rural island, people loved country songs.Long story short, there are a decent amount of classic reggae recordings covering country songs. And I frickin love that stuff. With the right band and the right recording, the songwriting really shines. I think there's something to be learned there: perhaps looking at the world in a different way can show us how very different groups of listeners have a lot in common after all.
label: critique

text: I don't hate country because of rednecks, I hate country because it is one of the least adventurous styles of music. It revels in musical repetition and cliches. It is legitimately one of the closest things to traditional rock and roll, because it uses the same chords and the same lyrical styles most of the time, but it hasn't progressed. Cheesy as fuck imo.And I will say that as a musician, I have seen many many non-country musicians suddenly put on the drawl and write much more basic music, about a "country" they do not know at all, and getting more successful because of it. I can respect them doing that to have a career, but I don't have to like the music.For years in my country anyone who tried to put on an accent whilst rapping was ridiculed and laughed out of town. However that criticism never comes against country. So I guess it goes both ways.
label: criticism

text: I very much disagree that country hasn’t progressed. There are many different sub genres and styles within country that have developed over the years. More to the point, the country music that has been most popular in recent years sounds very different to the country that was popular in the sixties or seventies. The fact that it has fused so successfully with pop and hip hop alone is evidence that it’s progressed. Now that’s not necessarily to say that I’m a big fan of this particular form of progression, but to deny that it’s happened seems a bit ridiculous.To your point about country music being cliched, it’s important to remember that songwriting about working class, mostly rural life is the heart of the genre. If you got rid of that it wouldn’t really be country anymore. But I think that in itself gives enormous scope for creativity in how this lifestyle is described and communicated. Yes, a lot of it involves the same tired cliches about beer, boobs, trucks, guns etc, but there are also songs of righteous political fury, personal regret and pain, beautiful love songs, and existential wondering that all fall under this umbrella. Hell I’d say Tyler Childers regularly covers all four of these things while staying true to country’s core subject matter. Check out Hard Times or Long Violent History for the first, Tattoos or Follow You To Virgie for the second, Lady May or Feathered Indians for the third, and Universal Sound for the last.Finally, I’d say that not every genre needs to progress to be good. Colter Wall is one of the most popular and acclaimed alternative country artists today, yet his music is pretty resolutely traditionalist and simplistic in its approach. Sometimes rediscovering a genre’s roots can give it a new lease on life, like the emergence of punk rock in the 70s and its mainstream breakout as grunge in the 90s.
label: critique

text: I personally won’t support or listen to racist music. Once an artist crosses that line it’s an easy decision for me. I lose all respect. Btw, this discussion has been going on for decades within the punk community with regards to Skrewdriver and their early singles and LP “All Screwed Up”. Basically the debate (usually a vicious argument actually) boils down to is it okay to own that LP. That record doesn’t give any hint as to what was to come from the band (same singer with different lineup) which was full on meat head white power bullshit. It’s also, and I hate to describe it that way, a good punk record. Do I think everyone who owns that record is racist? No I don’t. I will agree it doesn’t have any overt racist lyrics. Do I wonder if that person is an annoying contrarian ass just looking for an argument? Yes I do. I personally cracked my copy in half over 20yrs ago. I just changed my mind I guess. Got older and realized I do not want that record in my collection. Bad Brains are another example. And this one is probably the most disappointing and heartbreaking to me. I got the first record in 1986 when I was in high school and it had a HUGE impact on me. 20 years down the line I hear stories about HR and the band being total homophobes and really ugly stories about them. I don’t listen to the Bad Brains anymore. The stories were too much. Lost all respect and I can’t separate it. Maybe others can and that’s fine I guess? I don’t know. You can read up on that in the book American Hardcore by Steven Blush.
label: critique

text: The other thing about historical operas is that they continue to exist as living theater today. They aren't necessarily presented as historical objects,but rather as aesthetic objects that are still actively consumed to this day. Similar to Shakespeare.There are racist codes in Wagner's operas, that is clear. But there is an added layer in the fact that they are always newly staged and interpreted. Those new stagings may alternatively try to hide the racist elements and pretend they don't exist, or they may present them in a manner that calls attention to them and asks the audience to critique and think about them, or they may just be oblivious to them or portray those elements in a clumsy, insensitive way.Historical artwork can be useful in spurring new conversations about race and racism. But the question is how does one perform it in such a way that you make those conversations happen, rather than just thoughtlessly perpetuating Wagner's own values.
label: critique

text: Not a Taylor hate post - I’m asking sincerely and in good faith.She’s perfectly lovely in interviews and seems a good role model for girls. She is likeable and seems to carry her success well.I’m just trying to understand how she could’ve reached such astronomical heights of success when her music is comparatively so … bland?Not hating, I’ve genuinely tried to pin point her appeal.She is a pop culture phenomenon without being outstanding in terms of her music, singing, dancing, originality, looks or sex appeal. Anyone could see why they stormed pop culture. They were a marketers’ dream but they had truly great pop songs to back it up.But what explains the Taylor Swift phenomenon?Does anyone else feel like her super mega star status is a bit strange?

text: A big part of that parasocialism is also just due to how long she has been able to remain successful. text: I am a big fan. I have an answer though that hasn't been endlessly explored. The pandemic. Yes, she was a major star before the pandemic, but the album she released prior indicated a career in decline, the singles and videos weren't really connecting. Lover was not doing what past albums had done. Simultaneously she appeared in one of the most panned films of this century, Cats. The pandemic provided a reset at the exact right time. She brilliantly pivoted; made introspective, quiet and thoughtful albums, and created the events that we all needed while we were locked down. One of the most incredible uses of that time and situation.It wasn't merely that; Folklore and Evermore happen to be astounding pieces of art - it's not like she released something shoddy. She released two masterpieces, but in its way the lockdown bolstered her career and erased the missteps from everyone's mind.
label: appreciation

text: I think the main reason people don't listen to classical is most of it is not as readily appealing for modern sensibilities as current popular music, and much of the current stuff being written isn't being pushed for the masses by the music industry or the culture.Music is a language. To understand it you need exposure to it first. Play a completely new genre to someone, there's a good chance they'll think it all sounds the same. It takes exposure and active listening to understand new kinds of music.'Classical' is a tradition with many kinds, spanning many countries and cultures, with many trends and time periods. Just because you understand what feeling you're supposed to get out of a 19th century romantic piece from Vienna, doesn't mean you'll understand a Choral piece from the Italian Renaissance, or a current Holy Minimalist work from Estonia.When you're told these are the Greats you have to listen to, it's like doing homework where each question jumps around to completely different parts of a 2000 page textbook. If you've been to exposed your whole life by having parents listen to it or if you play it as part of learning an instrument, you'll have already made headway. Most people don't have this, so there's no real anchor point for them to start.
label: critique

text: Haydn was a self-taught peasant. Beethoven came from a down-and-out family and his dad was always in and out of jail. Bach was a poor orphan who had to steal blank sheet music in his teens because he couldn't afford it.Let's get on to Brahms. His family lived in a slum. They were so famously destitute that he had to supplement the family income by playing the piano in a whorehouse at the ripe old age of 11. Or Chopin, whose parents were servants and who couldn't even afford a piano teacher - yet he grew up to be one of the greatest piano composers (and teachers) in history.And let's talk about the highest paid concert pianist of the 19th century. You're probably picturing some well-to-do white guy in a powdered wig. Actually, the highest paid concert pianist of the 19th century had a double claim to fame - he is believed to have been the last legally owned slave in the USA. His name was Tom Wiggins, a black slave born on a plantation who was completely blind and was even named legally insane. Yet he loved classical music and had a repertoire of over 7000 pieces. And he was the highest paid concert pianist of the 19th century.In fact, prior to the 20th century, it was an extreme social taboo for the upper classes to seek a career in music. The most notable pre-20th century upper class composer I am aware of was the very talented Prince Ferdinand of Prussia. Ever heard of him? Thought not. And he's the most notable pre-20th century upper class composer I can think of. Even during the 20th century, many composers came from lives of terrible misfortune; Messiaen composed one of the 20th century's greatest pieces of classical music while living in a concentration camp during WWII.Classical music is a working class and middle class story. It's music of the people. Yes, rich people might have paid for a lot of it, but it tells the emotions and hardships of ordinary people. It's not for snobs. It's a 1000 year old recorded testament of the inner emotional lives of generations of working class and middle class people. To dismiss it as irrelevant music for snobs is - without any kind of hyperbole - a crime against history. You might as well demolish the pyramids of Giza under the illusion that they speak only for the pharaohs they were built for - when in fact they more meaningfully tell the story of the workers who built them. Don't let anti-intellectualism and reverse snobbery destroy our heritage.We seem to live in a culture where any tradition from before the 1950s is viewed as "posh". This also goes for great literature and the visual arts. The truth is that many of the greatest poets, novelists, painters and composers in history had horrific lives. Just look at Bach, an orphan who was widowed twice and had to bury 11 of his children. That's why these artists spent so much time trying to depict the breadth of human emotion. They felt something and they wanted to share it. We need to stop depicting these people as upper class boogeymen.I am absolutely sick of propaganda that seeks to deprive the working and middle classes from their cultural heritage. That's what this inverted snobbery against classical music is. Ordinary people composed it, and it is owned equally by every single member of the human race. That's the gift they gave us. Please don't throw it away in the name of some misguided attempt at reverse snobbery.
label: critique

text: Well first of all, jazz is huge. It's like saying you're not sure about rock, when rock runs the gamut from Elvis Presley to Tool. We're gonna do decades of jazz in a few songs here, so don't get mad when I skip over some stuff. As far as catchy hooks go, start with the easy stuff like Benny Goodman's 'Sing, Sing, Sing' and Janis Siegel's version of 'Bei Mir Bist Du Schön'. There's a hook, there's a rhythm, there's something you're humming on your way to work. Now we introduce improvisation. It's not like this didn't exist before jazz came along, but jazz is where it really came to life as a feature, a reason to go and see this musician instead of that one. Let's start with 'C Jam Blues', this is one we often use to introduce new students to jazz performance. The melody literally couldn't be simpler, it's two notes and a shuffle rhythm, this particular song only exists as a structure to play songs over. What feels good about this? It's the creativity of watching each player take the basic structure of the song and create something completely new, something that'll never happen quite the same way ever again, but still sounds as if it was always part of the original song. If you love the guitar solos in Van Halen or Phish or Dragonforce, this is where that came from. So, for years, jazz was about playing the biggest, baddest-ass, most creative, most complicated solo you could cook up, over top of a basic melody. Then Miles comes along with 'Kind Of Blue' and fucks everybody's head right up with the idea that maybe jazz improvisation isn't about how many complicated notes you can cram into 64 bars, maybe you can just sort of relax and explore and it's still going to blow people's minds. 'Freddie Freeloader' is still an absolutely basic melody that basically only exists to create a structure for solos, but what's different and (at the time) totally batshit crazy, is that Miles isn't trying to fill every second of solo time up with the craziest notes he can play, he's giving room to the space between the notes and setting flourishes of music off against calculated pauses to create a whole new feeling. On the same song, listen to the two different sax solos, Coltrane from 4:27-5:55 and Cannonball Adderly from 6:16-7:44. Trane leads off and plays a more traditional jazz style, where he's kind of showing off a little bit at first, but then he starts to relax and lets each phrase have a little more room to breathe. Cannonball comes in quieter, like he's not trying to impress quite so much, and gives it a completely different sound, but both sax solos are still throwing heavy heat when you compare it to the laid-back cool Miles started with. Does this make some sense?
label: appreciation

text: Older jazz aficionados hated fusion in its time and it has taken a while for appreciation to grow. It's some of my personal favorite stuff from the very broad genre of jazz. Checks out, gotta keep the damn kids off your lawn

text: While I think the music theory aspect of Nirvana can be interesting, I don’t know that it gets you any closer to the answer of why Nirvana became Nirvana (and why somebody else with a better grasp of songwriting did not). I think the theory angle, though intriguing on paper, doesn’t supersede historicity. Nirvana were the right band for the era and you could do everything they did musically today and not get anywhere because we are in a different place in history. The context really matters.Similarly, I think understanding the impact of Carly Rae Jepsen, particularly among a very male and very online audience, has much more to do with outside forces than her music and that context is crucial to understanding the listenership. Her music was when it became permissible for the Fantano audience to listen to and enjoy pop music. I already know this is a dicey metaphor but Jepsen is like the Juneteenth of poptimism: it was when word arrived to the last holdouts that it’s okay to listen to pop.
label: critique

text: My Apple Music served me up a bunch of Arcade Fire tonight and I started wondering about their body of work. I am looking for sincere opinions about this.Are any of their albums significant? As in, did they move the needle culturally? Were they emotionally resonant? Did they hit a certain demographic at exactly the right time and make fans for life?I think I may have been too old for their music to resonate with me when it came out. At the same time, I feel like the band tries to elicit nostalgia.The Suburbs sounds to me like the sonic equivalent of Stranger Things. It hits the right notes but it’s more of a throwback to something significant than it is something of significance in and of itself. Their music just feels empty, in a way, even though it had all the trappings of being emotional.
