text: Don't get me wrong, I like Beyoncé just fine and I think she is arguably one of the best performers of the 21st century and people tend to underestimate her prowess as a vocalist (she's easily the best vocalist of her generation). However, I find it so contrived with how her fanbase and many critics put her in an artistic pedestal despite being notorious in this department considering it's no industry secret that majority of her catalogue exists because of the shit ton of songwriters and producers that work for her.The issue is not collaboration - no artist is expected to be a self-made entity who writes and produces their songs own their own. But we can't deny that there’s this perpetual that Beyoncé is an artistic genius behind everything she touches but that doesn't really translate to reality given with what happens behind the scenes. For instance, adding her name to a track's credits after changing a single lyric or two doesn’t make her a songwriter in the traditional sense, yet she likes to claim that she plays a huge part in this, as well as the process of creating the concept for her music.And no, I'm not saying that singers are required to write their own stuff. A lot of musical legends such as Elvis Presley and Whitney Houston didn't really write their own songs (perhaps due to restrictions by their management), as well as Aretha Franklin who mostly sang covers (but was a prolific musician and arranger). The crux of the matter is that Beyoncé is shamelessly claiming part of songwriting royalties even though she really didn't write anything. I honestly wouldn't take issue of Beyoncé if only people praised her the same way people praise them but that's not the case. Not to mention, she also constantly takes credit for a lot of things other people have done such as her choreographies (eg. Single Ladies), claiming as though she created these dance routines.This is probably why I find it hard to respect her as an artist despite her being a great vocalist/performer. People love to shit on other singers such as Taylor Swift for a lot of her cringe ass lyrics but at least she writes her own songs and she deserves much more respect as an artist for actually writing her own music and perhaps constructing the concepts for her music on her own. All of this also made me understand why Beyoncé gets a lot of flak by being called overrated a lot. She's pretty much a watered down version of her influences such as Tina Turner and Etta James who has far more soul than she does, and they also happen to have written a huge chunk of their music, unlike Beyoncé. She feels the incessant need to portray this image of her being an untouchable and well-rounded elite in the music industry and being known as a singer who falls short from that (considering that she doesn't really write any of her songs nor devise the concept for her music) is perhaps an insecurity of hers.
label: criticism

text: We talk plenty about streaming here as the main threat to music and although I am thinking this is still mostly true, I came away with a different feeling these past years: it just got way way too pricey to take a wrong turn. I was reading about the death of new sounds\classics and although its specifically about the electronic music-scene, it did confirm some suspicions about the connection of everything to capitalistic crushes.Inflation and touring costs became insane, we know this, but also a flop album can smoke somebody's momentum in a year. Instant gratification in an age of lazy consumers is a killer – does it spook musicians into yielding to easy moves?The economy does not look like its going to be unfucked any time soon. Even DIY musicians need to play the money game now and for a fan, maybe you blow out 30 % your pay stub on a bum show one time, so you dont go exploring ever again.So I wonder if no safety net + algorithms + affordability crisis = doom.Is this a modern problem? And is this a really bad problem too, do you think?
label: criticism

text: For instance, I've read interviews with cartoonists and poets. Where they answer questions in a much more straightforward manner. Like someone you would meet in your regular life. They don't seem to hide their education, knowledge, or the work they had to do to get to where they are. However, the same is not true of music. It appears mainstream musicians have a "marketing team" that teaches them how to give interviews. I've only learned about this concept recently from reading articles from journalists.For example, some journalists will say that some bands have "image consultants" who help them craft their stories. For instance, I have read interviews where the musicians will present "zero to hero" narratives. They were completely broke, but then someone discovered their mixtape, and they were given a chance. All the sudden, their songs or song becomes popular by "chance" and then people like them due to their talent. It makes sense that mainstream musicians might create narratives like this since at the end of the day their goal is to sell records or sell concert tickets.I suppose their more mainstream audience wants to related to them. However, I am disappointing at certain "indie musicians." For instance, I've read an interview with an indie musician, and I was disappointed at how exaggerated and unbelievable her story was. She said something along that lines that she wasn't a singer but a friend told her that she should sing. Then she agreed with her friend and then she started uploading videos to social media. Without assuming that she could become known. But all the sudden, people started to take attention to her songs and then it became popular and she got to a record deal. To make her story more unbelievable, she said that to this day she barely knows any music theory, proficiency in piano, and guitar. But she writes based on how she "feels."To me this story sounds very implausible because "organic growth" in social media is actually very hard. In fact, the most honest answer is that many content creator actually pay social media sites to promote their content. And even then, for "popularity" to happen is unlikely. What happens is that when you pay, you might get some fans or customers, but that's about it. There's no guarantee that any of your content might become "viral." So this is more like a business where you put in some money to promote your work and get some returns. What do you think?
label: criticism

text: I'm not sure posts of the form 'does anybody else hate blank?' really contribute to a discussion of music. Yes, somebody else hates whatever it is you hate. Congratulations. I've found most love songs to be poorly written and generic. I mean, what's the saying? 90% of everything is garbage? Most pop songs could be considered poorly written and generic, whether they're about love, partying, being spurned (does this count as a love song, or its opposite?), how wonderful life is, how shit life is... these are universal themes. So of course they're generic because they're trying to appeal to a broad audience. There are many amazing love songs. Look at Paul McCartney. 'Maybe I'm Amazed' is a fantastic love song. So is, though I might get pushback on this one, 'Silly Love Songs.' (Which is lampshading your complaint by acknowledging it, doing it anyway, and doing it better than most of the rest.) I think love songs work best when they're more specific, to be sure. Look at stuff like Jukebox the Ghost's 'Fred Astaire,' or Whitehorse's 'Achilles' Desire.' They're well-written and specific. I also have a soft spot for Eric Clapton's 'Wonderful Tonight.' Honestly, I could come up with 100 great love songs that I... er, love. I guess maybe love songs are intended for people who have perfect lives and are already in love. Love songs can be really grating if you're lonely, or just broke up with somebody. Get yourself a few of The Smiths albums or some early Morrissey, or some good blues if you're feeling melancholy.
label: critique

text: I've been undertaking a project of listening to the biggest pop hits of each year, which has been a lot of fun. That is, until I started listening to music from the 00s, music that was released when I was already an adult. There is hard switch that happens for me, I can't overlook the flaws in music from the 00s as a product of their time. For example, if I listen to 'Sugar, Sugar' by the Archies, I see it as the campy, unserious song it is. If I listen to 'Hey, Soul Sister' by Train, there is no mirth, no feeling that it's a playful, carefree song. All I see is blind rage. Fuck that song. I find that I'm much more forgiving of music from the distant past, as if those older musicians didn't know better and Train should have known better. And I recognize that other people probably have this same line somewhere in their psyche where music of one era is a romp and music of another era is evaluated much more seriously, but that line is located in a completely different spot. I get that there is a very lewronggeneration slant to this post, but I don't think that is (completely) it. Perhaps if I was born in the 40s, I'd have a rosy view of Delta Blues music and I would hate all hippie music. I also wonder if an aspect of people thinking all new music sucks is related to this feeling I have. Maybe music started sucking for people when they began looking at the world around them through the eyes of an adult. Maybe Train just sucks.
label: criticism

text: I don't like the childish approach. If done intentionally for a few individual songs with matching subject matter it can work, but when that's all of their music, I just get the feeling that they only know how to make childish music. If every album you make is about 'viewing adulthood and the struggles of growing up through the lens of childhood,' then it comes cross as creatively stagnant. You're just doing the same thing over and over. Their electronic, frenzied style comes across as discordant a lot of the time. 'Three-Thirty' is perhaps their biggest offender out of the songs I've heard. The few songs by them that I think are okay are 'Sober Up,' 'Dear Winter,' 'Role Models,' and the original 'Turning Out.' All of which are relatively straightforward and serious songs that aren't constantly trying to wink in your face with their craaaaaaaazy references. Their worst, IMO, are 'Birthday Party' (which is perhaps the single worst song I've ever heard in all my days on this earth), 'Three-Thirty,' 'Beats,' and '100 Bad Days.' They have yet to master the art of a half-decent beat drop. I don't think that unfiltered word-vomit lyrics, no matter how sincere the sentiments they carry, make for good art. I think some degree of refinement is necessary. I admire their commitment to emotional honesty in their music, but it works against them more than it works for them. For reference, some of my favorite artists are The Smiths, Radiohead, Mitski, The Mountain Goats, Joyce Manor, and Bassvictim.
label: criticism

text: I've been seeing a lot of praise for Olivia's new album with very little critique at all so i wanted to share ny thoughts because my view seems to be very different. I see many saying things such as ""this album is good for what it is"" but I do believe this album wasn't even that good for what it was supposed to be. The concept had a lot more potential, but jumps right from drop dead girl crush to stupid song and it really has too much sad ballads which really just sound the same (first time I'm saying this, I usually hate the ""it sounds the same"" argument being said just because an album is sonically cohesive, because I love cohesive albums but these genuinely sound... kind of the same.) I feel that the amount of ballads and sad songs take away from the concept of trying to show the chapters of a relationship in different stages, because she was so into trying to incorporate the ""sad"" aspect of a love song that she failed to capture the highs almost completely. I'm not saying the album is bad, i am saying the album is atleast mediocre and at most good but at the very baseline. I do genuinely believe ""the cure"" is honestly one of the best songs released in recent times not just for Olivia. ""Drop dead"" is pretty bland and I didn't get the praise when it was released, everyone acting as if it's an insane, extraordinary pop song. The part where I truly lose my braincells is the praising of ""maturity"" on this album .. really? Maturity? There was a good deal of maturity in GUTS is what I will admit. I'm also a teenager but if anything it makes me more confused... did I really just see multiple people in their 30s, 40s, late 20s, whatever it is... say this album is mature? An album filled with angsty teenager songs like maggots for brains (shes a ""sad shell of a woman when her baby is away""), my way, (""here's the part where the girl gets pissed, the girls is me, you're in my way now""), drop dead (stalked you on the Internet, kiss me and I might drop dead, it's feminine intuition), expectations and a bunch of basic teen sadgirl ballads (not meant to be a bad thing but people calling it mature is getting to my brain) like purple, what's wrong with me (he's whats wrong with her, wow), less (very predictable meaning, wish he loved her less if it means he wont leave her), but no apparently the critics think it's mature too so im thinking bias? The thing is I'm not saying it has to be mature. I don't understand this entire ""maturity"" discourse, let her write how she wants, she's like what.. 23? But the fact is it is NOT mature at all. One of the most teenager albums ever released. With GUTS I could understand. It has lacy, making the bed, the grudge, pretty isn't pretty and vampire which are well written and cover themes through a good lens. Even SOUR was more mature with jealousy jealousy, brutal and 1 step forward 3 steps back. I feel like everyone is using the cure as the single example for this album being ""mature."" I usually don't like cherrypicking lyrics from an album to act like the rest of the thing is bad but you can pick out a line that makes you go ""Oh wow..."" from probably almost every song on the album. The aesthetics also feel very... it feels like she made a pinterest board and decided to run with that and force this new softer, feminine persona except it just doesn't feel natural. And please don't get me started with all the rock references. Atleast the cure is a good song but I get it, you're so very cultured. You like weezer, courtney love, the cure and you know all the drinking games. Has it ever been more common for singers to create an irritating personality along with their new music? They've always done it but it used to feel like the music was a reflection of THEIR current persona rather than THEM trying to be this forced version of the MUSIC instead. It's even crazier once you realise the album isn't even giving alcohol or rock girl anyway. Atleast she nailed the pinterest aesthetics?
label: criticism

text: I’ve noticed that when I read interviews with cartoonists and poets, they tend to answer questions in a straightforward, relatable manner—like someone you would meet in everyday life. They don't seem to hide their education, knowledge, or the hard work it took to get where they are.However, the same cannot be said for the music industry. It appears that mainstream musicians rely heavily on marketing teams and image consultants to teach them how to handle interviews and craft their public narratives. I frequently see musicians present a 'zero to hero' backstory: they claim they were completely broke until someone randomly discovered their mixtape, leading to overnight popularity entirely by 'chance.' While it makes financial sense for mainstream artists to manufacture these relatable narratives to sell records and concert tickets, I find it deeply disappointing when indie musicians do the same.Recently, I read an interview with an indie musician whose story felt completely exaggerated and implausible. She claimed she wasn't even a singer until a friend encouraged her to try it. According to her, she began uploading videos to social media with zero expectations, only to instantly go viral and land a record deal. To make her story even more unbelievable, she claimed that she still barely knows any music theory or how to play the piano or guitar, insisting that she writes music purely based on how she 'feels.'This kind of narrative sounds highly unrealistic because organic growth on social media is incredibly difficult to achieve. In reality, most content creators have to pay social media platforms to promote their work. Even then, popularity is never guaranteed; paying for promotion usually just yields a small, steady stream of fans or customers rather than a viral sensation. At the end of the day, it is a business where you invest capital to promote your work and hope for a return. What do you think?
label: criticism

text: It's just good old-fashioned sexism, and it isn't exclusive to music. Movies with a large female audience also tend to get derided, even spawning pejorative terms like 'women's weepers' or 'chick flicks.' The reason for this is actually quite simple: film criticism has long been, and continues to be, dominated by men. It is no coincidence that the vast majority of movies widely heralded as classics are exactly the type of films that appeal to those male critics.Have you noticed how few female characters there are in movies like The Godfather, Goodfellas, Apocalypse Now, Saving Private Ryan, The Dark Knight, Fight Club, The Matrix, 12 Angry Men, The Shawshank Redemption, Jaws, Schindler's List, Star Wars, Seven Samurai, Lawrence of Arabia, The Bridge on the River Kwai, 2001: A Space Odyssey, and A Clockwork Orange? Furthermore, there are numerous acclaimed directors who rarely make movies about women, such as Akira Kurosawa, Martin Scorsese, Wes Anderson, and Christopher Nolan.Music criticism has faced the exact same male-dominated bias. It is no coincidence that lists of the greatest music artists are consistently dominated by men: The Beatles, The Rolling Stones, The Beach Boys, Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin, Queen, Jimi Hendrix, Chuck Berry, Elvis Presley, Bob Dylan, Johnny Cash, Willie Nelson, Hank Williams, Waylon Jennings, Eminem, Jay-Z, Tupac Shakur, The Notorious B.I.G., Kendrick Lamar, Michael Jackson, David Bowie, Bruce Springsteen, James Brown, Ray Charles, Nirvana, Radiohead, Oasis, Miles Davis, John Coltrane, Duke Ellington, and Bob Marley.Don't get me wrong—I'm not saying any of these movies or music artists are bad. And sure, we can all think of exceptions like Alien, Titanic, Dolly Parton, or Joni Mitchell. However, it would be much more difficult for anyone to compile a list of 100 great movies without any male leads than a list of 100 great movies without any female leads. It's not that excellent female-led films don't exist; rather, they aren't as widely known, and very few are regarded as 'classics' like the titles mentioned above. This discrepancy is even more apparent in music. Despite the hundreds of well-known female singers and musicians, most people would still struggle to create a list of 100 great music artists consisting entirely of women, whereas creating an all-male list would be effortless.On a broader level, genres associated with femininity—like melodramas, romantic comedies, disco, or dance-pop—tend to be overlooked or dismissed as lightweight and frivolous, unworthy of serious discussion or evaluation. As a result, even men associated with these genres are held in lower regard, such as directors Douglas Sirk and Garry Marshall, or virtually any boy band in music history.Who gets to decide what is worthy? Individually, any of us can and should decide for ourselves. But on a societal level, critics wield far greater influence, and the fields of film and music criticism remain heavily male-dominated. A telling example of this is how a publication like Pitchfork ignored Taylor Swift for years, yet didn't hesitate to review a track-by-track cover album of her music recorded by a lesser-known male indie-rock singer, Ryan Adams. Even though it was a negative review, it highlights a stark reality: Swift was already a massive star at the time but wasn't considered worthy of a review, while a man recording her exact songs was. It makes you think about how many lesser-known female artists get completely ignored by male critics who deem them unworthy of even a bad review.
label: criticism

text: MJ was essentially the first global pop icon. That is, his peak as performer coincided with major technological and economic developments, namely the globalization of media and branding, as well as the end of the Cold War, which opened new audiences and markets for him and his image as part of American feel-good capitalism. MJ became known around the world through his music videos featured on MTV, and as spokesman for products like Pepsi.
label: appreciation

text: First and foremost, hip-hop is the culture that encompasses rap music; there is no distinction between a genre called 'hip-hop music' and a separate 'rap music.' As KRS-One famously said, 'Rap is something you do; hip-hop is something you live.' Largely, today’s rap lacks respect for its roots and the culture as a whole. There is a pervasive sentiment that many of today's artists rap solely for the money rather than out of love for the culture. Even the most popular gangster rappers of the 90s would speak about emulating Run-DMC or LL Cool J; you rarely hear that kind of reverence from artists today.This lack of respect is what strikes me most. Many modern artists will gladly sample an old rap beat while simultaneously trashing their predecessors. This makes the sampling feel like a blatant money grab born of a lack of creativity, rather than an act of homage to those who came before them. Moreover, the simplicity of today's lyrics is not the real issue—early rap was often just as simple in its rhyme structure—but there was an individuality, character, and creativity then that is missing from the majority of popular rap today. Too $hort did not need complicated rhymes to make great music, but when he emerged, there was no one else like him. He was truly unique in his content, voice, and production.There used to be a heavy emphasis on unique, regional sounds. You could distinguish an artist’s origin by their style—California, Texas, Georgia, or New York—and even within those regions, you could identify the specific crew. Cypress Hill did not sound like the Dogg Pound; the Dogg Pound did not sound like Freestyle Fellowship; Freestyle Fellowship did not sound like The Alkaholiks. In contrast, today, artists like Roddy Ricch and Young Thug sound remarkably similar.There was a time when being a clone would get an artist shunned. Take Shyne, for example; he was initially hated by many for sounding like Biggie. Artists like Tha Realest, Krazy, Guerilla Black, Sicario, and Angelous were treated as blatant rip-offs. Today, mirroring another artist's sound is considered a fast track to success.It is telling that every time this argument arises, the same two names are almost always cited: J. Cole and Kendrick Lamar, with the occasional mention of Lil Baby, Joey Bada$$, or Griselda. In the 90s, the list of heavy hitters included Tupac, Biggie, Snoop, Nas, Method Man, OutKast, Ice Cube, Eminem, Common, Jay-Z, Scarface, Redman, Raekwon, Ghostface, and Bone Thugs-N-Harmony. Every one of them made albums that stood out and hold up to this day.I also disagree that modern beats are more creative or diverse. Nearly every sound currently popular takes major influence from DJ Paul and Juicy J’s 90s production, as well as the influence of DJ Screw. If you believe today’s rap is more creative than the work of DJ Quik, Madlib, Pete Rock, RZA, Muggs, or Mike Dean, I believe your perspective is flawed.Lyrically, there is absolutely less creativity and diversity today—it is not even close. There are no artists currently operating like Del the Funky Homosapien or MF DOOM. Even mid-tier rappers from the 90s or early 2000s, like 2Mex or Papoose, were significantly more creative than those topping the charts today. There is no modern equivalent to Prince Paul’s A Prince Among Thieves. Big Pun was rapping bar-for-bar with Black Thought, crafting street narratives with Kool G Rap, and delivering pop hits with J.Lo—you simply do not see that kind of range today.You made many general statements without providing examples. I would love to hear specific comparisons from both today and the past so I can better understand how you arrived at your conclusions.
label: criticism

text: Classical is my first choice for passive listening, having something on in the background that I will catch bits and pieces of. Or turn up the volume if it compels me to do so. For both living space audio and in the car it's usually calming and thought-provoking, even if just subconsciously. Except for the beg-a-thons on the local classical FM station which mine is in the midst of, those tend to annoy me more than another format of station would because I have gotten used to the lack of speaking or singing, except for opera which is sort of an offshoot and not really the same.Active listening I do via headphones usually and that's more me hunting out specific songs to listen to, or playing clips I saved. It wasn't until I was into my 40s that I recognized the difference between actively listening to music and doing so passively, and how that creates different reactions in my brain and body.
label: appreciation

text: Yes, all the time. I am largely in this subreddit to get a feel for a popular music landscape I am less educated in, but I also like to remind people that there is a wider world of music out there. This includes musical values not limited to the largely American, rock-and-hip-hop-centric conceptions of what music should be.I think the reason for the decline of classical music is very simple: paying orchestras to rehearse and perform a 40-minute symphony—after years of grueling training—for audiences who have very specific, opinionated ideas about what constitutes a 'good' version of that symphony, is far more expensive for capitalists than simply marketing a single 'artist.' It is much easier to construct a story around an individual, and their three-minute songs are easier to market heavily. Consequently, this lack of exposure results in a snowball effect. Sadly, unlike another user, I believe this drop in interest is very real.Fortunately, I can say that many users on this sub are very open to different musical experiences, and there are quite a few of us who are well aware of the significance of this tradition (though there was one person, a very young user, who once loudly stated that they wished to discourage any talk of older music on this sub).
label: appreciation

text: My favorite Comfort Eagle song is definitely the title track. I like deeper, growly, darker sounds, though, so the guitar throughout scratches that itch for me. The lyrics are great, and that sitar/banjo/whatever part . . . perfection. I would counter that Cake isn't necessarily humorous all the time (though you did say ""different levels""). In fact, I've found lots of their tracks to be fabulous background music for being generally bummed out. ""Sad Songs and Waltzes,"" ""World of Two,"" and one of my all-time favorites, ""Friend is a Four-Letter Word"" fit the bill, among plenty of others. I'm a sad song connoisseur, though, so of course these attract my attention. I didn't realize there was an opinion out there that Comfort Eagle topped the band's discography in either technique or popularity. When you pull them up on Spotify, Fashion Nugget is the first album listed in their ""Popular Releases"" section. I have to admit, they had kind of faded from my memory by the time Comfort Eagle was released, and even though ""No Phone"" was a hit, I couldn't tell you anything else about Pressure Chief and know absolutely nothing about their 2011 Showroom of Compassion (though maybe I should fix that). For me, it's all about Fashion Nugget and Prolonging the Magic with dips into past and future for ""Jolene,"" ""Rock and Roll Lifestyle,"" and ""Comfort Eagle."" On the 1-10 scale, I'd rate the track ""Comfort Eagle"" a solid 9, but the whole album would maybe come in around 7. This could be a little bit of style fatigue, though, because as you said, their sound never evolved much with a few notable exceptions. I loved their earlier stuff, and didn't necessarily need a whole bunch of the same unless something about the music or lyrics was extra special.
label: appreciation

text: I discovered them a few years late, after hearing Johnny Marr praise them in an instagram post. Their sound, frankly, made me feel young again. I used to be obsessed with discovering new artists, but by the time I finished university I basically had an established roster I was happy to rotate before seeking out new stuff. I also thought that the four-five piece rock band was basically dead: a few old guard artists still tour but were losing edge with age, while newer bands redo a lot of well-trod terrain. So when I turned this on, it hit me like brick. The very first song storms out of the gate at full speed, with Grian shouting at the mic like he’s leading a protest march. At the chorus he declares: “My childhood was small, but I’m gonna be big!” Simple, blunt lyrics backed by a simple, blunt riff. I’m instantly flooded with memories of my own ambitions and shoulder chips as a hothead young man. Oh, to be that cocky and confident again. The instruments behind him have a slight punk edge, but still play very tight and deliberately. It feels both old and fresh, original yet nostalgic. Over the rest of the album, they stay on the tight rope. The sound and pacing is consistent with occasional mosh pit flareups followed by melodic deceleration. Then they bookend the whole thing with a complete curveball, slowing down to croon a steady, tear-jerking Irish folk ballad that they wrote themselves yet sounds like something that could have been chanted in pubs for centuries. It remains an album that I feel compelled to listen to start to finish every time. Everything is in exactly the right place. My full range of emotions get activated. Feelings I haven’t had in years come back. I love their subsequent releases too, and was very impressed that each album manages to sound unique yet internally consistent. They’ve gotten comfortable testing new sounds without ever seeming too indulgent or deviating too far from their core competencies. But Dogrel is my last great love because it helped me fall back in love with music.
label: appreciation

text: Country has always been pop. Or at least there has always been 2 camps of country music. The Nashville machine began as soon as technology gave us the ability to record and distribute music. Now you could argue even that older pop country was more genuine and "real" country. But what we've seen happen to mainstream country is just a slow evolution of something that started in the 40s and 50s.
label: appreciation

text: I think a lot of it is also that a lot of the romanticized country lifestyle (cowboys, outlaws running moonshine, coal miners, etc.) doesn't really exist anymore in the modern world. Even farming, while it still exists, is mostly done by machines and most of the workers are migrants, not handsome white dudes with perfect tans. You still see a lot of pop country artists doing songs about farming but I'd guarantee 90% of them have never been on a tractor or planted a crop, they're just pandering to (mostly) people who never have done that either but romanticize this idea of farming. You still hear a lot of alt country artists sing about stuff that's more realistic and relatable to rural folk but some of it is more dark than romantic (songs about the opioid crisis hitting rural WV brutally, growing up poor in a trailer park, etc.)
label: criticism

text: Insane Clown Posse might be the most hated music group in modern history. More than Nickelback, more than Imagine Dragons, more than Limp Bizkit, more than any of them. Unlike those three, ICP NEVER had support from the media, and their fans were designated a gang. But even though musically the group is certainly not everyone's cup of tea, they are honestly a lot better than people give them credit for. They get judged harshly because they're not 'serious' rappers—what the hell do you expect from guys who dress up in clown makeup and spray Faygo over their audience? Illmatic Part 2? But they are great storytellers and have a solid sense of humor. Some songs I recommend are: '12' from Riddle Box. This song is a great concept song. It’s about a jury of 12 and a guy who was sentenced to death by them for a crime he didn’t commit. So he comes back to kill all 12 that condemned him. 'The Pendulum's Promise' from Bizaar. This song is about people judging others, and not realizing... they are also in many ways describing themselves. 'Crossing Thy Bridge' from The Wraith: Shangri La. This is borderline metal. It's about being horrified at seeing children die at a young age, and talking to God about wanting redemption. 'Halls Of Illusions' from The Great Milenko. Probably one of the closest things these guys have to a 'hit.' A great concept song about taking child abusers and woman beaters on a deadly ride representing a fun-house to reflect upon their actions, as they are shown the consequences of their ways as they pass through the ‘Halls of Illusions.’ If you want to check out those songs, do so and give ICP a chance. If you just blindly go by the 'ICP sucks' mentality that you're supposed to think because Eminem famously dissed them and Miracles went viral... more power to you. You're not gonna want to drink Faygo and paint your face up after listening to these tracks, but it could give you a solid look at the other side of the clowns.
label: appreciation

text: On paper, pairing mainstream pop king Jack Antonoff (Taylor Swift, Lana Del Rey, Bleachers) with ex-Bring Me The Horizon sound-design wizard Jordan Fish sounds completely unhinged. They operate in entirely different musical ecosystems, but under the hood, a collaboration actually makes perfect sense. Both are absolute production maximalists who specialize in creating massive, cinematic walls of sound.Imagine merging Antonoff's warm, pulsing, retro 80s analog synths and organic, stomping percussion with Fish's biting digital synth leads, glitchy electronic beats, and complex, heavily layered vocal production. It would be an incredibly rich, high-energy soundscape that retains a raw, emotional heartbeat.They wouldn't even need to form a band; just think of the absolute chaos they could cause as a co-producing duo. They could easily push a pop giant like Halsey, Olivia Rodrigo, or Billie Eilish into dark, heavy, industrial-pop territory, or pull a massive alternative band like Spiritbox, Bad Omens, or even BMTH themselves into pristine, stadium-sized pop focus.With Jordan Fish currently operating as a free agent and Antonoff always hunting for unexpected textures to keep his sound fresh, this crossover isn't a total pipe dream. The gap between heavy alternative music and mainstream pop has never been smaller, and a collab between these two titans would absolutely break the industry
label: appreciation

text: Since discovering Jimmy Cauty’s Space, I’ve become fascinated by the incredibly brief creative partnership between Cauty and Alex Paterson. It feels as though something special happened whenever these two crossed paths. They were the original incarnation of The Orb. Together they developed those early ambient DJ sets, worked on ‘A Huge Ever Growing Pulsating Brain That Rules from the Centre of the Ultraworld,’ and began the project that eventually became Space before their partnership fell apart. Then they separated, and this extraordinary cluster of music emerged around them: Space, Chill Out, and The Orb’s Adventures Beyond the Ultraworld. Obviously, the precise credits and chronology are complicated. Paterson has long claimed that he contributed significantly to Chill Out (on one podcast he claimed it was 80% him!), while Space was reportedly stripped of his contributions and reworked by Cauty following their split. There are competing accounts, bruised feelings, and probably no completely objective version of what happened. But I’m less interested in figuring out who made what percentage of each record than I am in the creative relationship itself. Listening to the original ‘Pulsating Brain,’ Space, Chill Out, and the early Orb material, I keep wondering whether Cauty and Paterson brought something unique out of each other. Perhaps Paterson supplied the endless curiosity, samples, strange juxtapositions, and free-associative flow, while Cauty brought a particular sense of shape, atmosphere, and conceptual scale. That is just interpretation, but together they seemed capable of making music that felt enormous, playful, mysterious, and completely outside ordinary time. Both men made brilliant work without each other, so this isn’t about diminishing either of them. But that short overlap feels almost alchemical. It helped establish a musical language that both The Orb and the KLF would then carry in different directions. Had they stayed together longer, would The Orb have become what it became? Would Chill Out exist in the same form? Would Space now be regarded as an ambient classic rather than an obscure KLF-adjacent curiosity? Or was the brevity and tension of their partnership exactly why that music was so special? I’d love to hear from people who know this period better. What do you think each of them brought to the partnership, and where can you hear it most clearly?
label: appreciation

text: Oddly enough, even though music is inherently something you hear, I can’t help but feel that being a musician and working out what a part or song might be yourself actually gives you a bit more of a firsthand visual and tactile immersion in what you like and are interested in. Of course, there’s no way to ever fully, completely, or even mostly understand the thought process of someone when composing their piece, but if you’ve paid particular attention to anything that sounded smooth or efficient—something with not much unnecessary movement, smooth voice leading, a line cliché, whatever—you can physically see and feel where that goes in addition to the usual aural processing of it. I have to believe that the more you educate or expose yourself to something, the more you begin to pick up on and notice things that just wouldn’t stand out to you, or wouldn’t seem like something you could identify or reference, outside of just knowing that you’re listening to a certain piece. Learning about structure, keys, major and minor, and rhythm gives you a sense of knowing what something is, and I think it gives you a little extra spring in your step to notice it when you hear it being used across different bits of music. It doesn’t take being a musician to appreciate music, but I do believe it’s almost like you’re appreciating something from a bit of a scholar's level rather than a passerby or from a casual, touring perspective. There is nothing wrong with the latter; you just know a bit more about what you are experiencing, and that can make the experience feel a bit more rewarding, I think. That being said, when I’m listening to music, especially for the first few times, I’m as green or as much a novice as someone who hasn’t been a musician. It’s a lot to take something in for the first time, and my mind doesn’t start to process things like keys, cadences, or counterpoint until well past the point when I start to get comfortable with what I’m hearing. You know how you feel when listening to music you like even as a non-musician, but as a musician, you might be able to articulate that feeling and have a more enriched awareness of what’s at play that makes you feel that way.
label: appreciation

Brand New Cadillac is fine but I'm not sure how this version substantially improves upon the Vince Taylor version. The Reggae/Ska tracks seem to substitute the original rhythmic of the genres with a fairly traditional rock beat that doesn't really fit. text: I don't know if this is a hot take, but I've been seeing a lot of videos on TikTok reacting to Sabrina Carpenter singing 'Like a Prayer' at Coachella this past weekend. Everyone is in total awe of how amazing she sounds, but it just reminded me how much I don't care for her voice. No hate to Sabrina at all—there's no denying how talented she is—but ever since she blew up, I've always felt like her voice is too 'perfect' or polished; it always sounds edited. Just to be clear, I'm not accusing her of anything, especially because the overwhelming majority of people in pop music clean up their vocals in the studio at least a little bit. If anything, it's a compliment to how accurate of a vocalist she is. But despite her talent, I just don't find her voice interesting or fun to listen to, whether it's live or in the studio. I feel the same way about Justin Bieber after watching some videos of his Coachella performances. He has a great voice, but again, it’s too clean and boring; I don't hear or feel any emotion behind it. There are plenty of singers—like Beyoncé, Stevie Wonder, or George Michael—who are also incredibly accurate and could be described as having 'perfect' voices, but in my opinion, there is actually feeling or soul behind them. I was just curious if anyone else feels the same way about these types of voices in general; if so, who else do you feel this way about? (For context, my all-time favorite is George Michael, and then in no particular order: Fiona Apple, Julian Casablancas, Chappell Roan, Clairo, and Alex Turner.) Edit: Okay, so this isn’t a hot take after all. I’m also realizing that maybe I just don’t like modern pop music that much, which makes more sense to me now.

text: So much has already been said about this album that I’m not sure what more we can add that the many excellent critics haven’t already covered. You criticize the instrumentation, but I think the playing is powerful, simple, and unique; the singing is rough and expressive, and the production is superb and detailed. Even if the songs themselves were undistinguished, it would be a pleasure to hear them play. However, with a couple of minor exceptions—I’ve never been crazy about 'Jimmy Jazz,' and 'Revolution Rock' and 'Lovers’ Rock' could be a little more distinct and compelling—the songs are first-rate. In addition to the ones you listed, 'Spanish Bombs,' 'Train in Vain,' 'Rudie Can’t Fail,' and the glorious 'Lost in the Supermarket' never fail to give me a charge, and the rest are excellent. So, I have to ask: what music do you like? Do you like other punk or older rock and roll? What about The Rolling Stones or the early Beatles? My guess is that your taste might resemble that of the other commenter who said that The Clash walked so Bauhaus—a band I can’t stand—could run. One thing London Calling isn’t is musically progressive; it is an album that embraces rock’s past without indulging in nostalgia. It demonstrates punk’s potential as it proves the durability of old forms like rockabilly, R&B, and ska. It situates punk in history, but that history is the history of American music—rock and roll and its Black precursor styles—not the European art tradition. There is pretty much no experimentation with classical music, no fancy harmonies, exotic instruments, or weird time signatures. This differentiates it from, say, later Beatles albums, Radiohead, or any of the dozens of albums you might be referencing when you compare the 'dull instrumentation' on London Calling to other 'all-time great albums.'
label: critique

text: I like physical media for all the same reasons everyone’s already said, but I’ve got a few more, too. I try to get most of my tapes, CDs, and vinyl from my local record stores. If it’s a special release that the stores aren’t going to carry or if it’s too obscure for my local shops, I’ll go to Discogs, the artist’s shop on Bandcamp, or their website; I feel this is better for my local economy and the artists' wallets. Going to those physical stores is fun, and I enjoy spending an hour or two digging through the inventory to find something special or cool. That’s half the fun of collecting—buying stuff online or streaming doesn’t scratch that itch for time-spending or the social aspect of talking to the employees. As someone who has struggled to reintegrate into society after a long spell of depression kept me inside, that social aspect is really good for me, even if it is painfully awkward a lot of the time. There is also a plethora of small bands and artists who are only releasing some music on cassette or other physical formats, and getting to own a piece of their art in that way is cool and makes me feel good. The last part is that I’ve found streaming music has affected my attention span, like everything else online apparently has. When I’m trying to work and listen to music through Spotify on my phone, I’m far more likely to want to skip around and then get distracted. Listening on my tape or CD players makes me more mindful and intentional in my listening habits, and I tend to enjoy the music more as well as remain focused, all without having to touch this little portal to hell.
label: appreciation

text: To spell that out musically and stylistically: K-pop is very tightly based on the 1990s Max Martin, Britney Spears, and Backstreet Boys production style. That is powerful stuff—perhaps the most effective style of pop music ever created. Even as a music nerd, I can’t deny it; that sound just works on you. It is the lasagna of pop music—just an MSG overload. J-pop, in comparison, is consciously weird and goofy. People often miss that because, on the surface, it is built on a commercial idol machine, leading them to group it with K-pop. But if you look closely, there is a weird, self-conscious irony in Japan, shared by both the fans and the producers. It isn't played with a straight face like K-pop; it is perhaps closer to something like hair metal, which J-pop explicitly took many cues from. The music is totally eclectic. They occasionally incorporate that Backstreet Boys sound, but the primary style is what we intuitively think of as 'anime music'—that high-energy, over-the-top, cheery sing-along style. That isn't just for anime; that is what the main idols do, too. Take the most played song by AKB48, for example. It is eclectic: the same idol will perform a ballad, then switch to ultra-sad Celine Dion-style orchestral string music. There are a lot of jazz elements, actually—diminished and complex chord progressions—alongside their famous penchant for 80s power metal guitar solos. You might see a mainstream J-pop idol band charting with a song that sounds like the Super Mario theme, featuring harmonies that are definitely not 'easy listening.' Japanese pop culture is a bit like that. It is a weird mix of over-the-top indulgence in archetypal fantasies, yet it maintains a bizarre, self-reflective awareness and irony in a highly eclectic blend. Another good comparison would be the artists selected for the Eurovision Song Contest. It is all over-the-top kitsch, yet it is simultaneously making fun of itself. Mainstream Japanese pop culture feels like one constant Eurovision contest. It isn't as straightforwardly teenage-driven or reliant on straight-faced sex appeal as K-pop; it is much more driven by Japanese variety shows, where you might see an idol sitting in a studio with 50-year-olds, rating their favorite insects on a scale of 1 to 10. As an idol, you are expected to have that kind of self-effacing humor and play along well in that sort of Saturday morning trash TV context—very similarly to how Eurovision performers establish their status.
label: critique

text: I think the main reason pre-60s pop vocals sound antiquated is that the conventions of the time were very traditional and conservative. Pop music as we know it emerged in the 50s as a completely new musical paradigm, yet its song structure, lyrical content, and melodic harmonies remained strongly tied to the traditional music from which early pop heavily borrowed. Pre-1960s pop vocal melodies tend to be tightly tethered to the melodic structure of the song, which are often based on simple, tonal chord progressions. Lyrically, songwriters then—just as they do today—strived to appeal to a broad audience by offering accessible and immediately relatable content. Pop of the 1960s, in large part, retained these fundamental characteristics. It is important to remember that the exemplars of the age are not representative of the whole; while groups like The Beatles and The Beach Boys were pushing the boundaries of commercial pop, many acts were satisfied simply with selling singles. For example, 1967 was the year The Beatles released Sgt. Pepper’s, yet the following year, the highest-charting songs included "Honey" by Bobby Goldsboro and "This Guy’s in Love With You" by Herb Alpert. I am sure neither of those songs are recognizable to a modern audience; in fact, they likely sound just as antique as anything from the 50s. I guess my point is that it is important to be fair about what the pop scene was really like, because although there were some timeless standouts, most songs were just pop songs like any other: simple, singable, danceable, predictable, and, most importantly, commercial.
label: critique

text: Hans Zimmer, John Williams, and their peers do not represent what "classical" music is today. Film music is an entirely different discipline than traditional composed music, owing much more to the 19th century than the 20th. Of course, much of that music was original to begin with, so it doesn't really apply to this discussion. It is clear from your post that you do not know much about the music you are discussing. Stating that classical music is "terrible" without providing any reasoning is hard to take seriously, and failing to define what you mean by "classical music" is highly problematic. I will disregard your first and third points because they are incorrect. While there is a perception that classical music is elitist, attending a concert would prove that notion wrong. Furthermore, the music is only as "uncomfortable" to listen to as the chair you are sitting in; that has nothing to do with the music itself. Unless you are watching opera or ballet, it is also very unlikely you will encounter a three-hour concert—usually, a performance is an hour to an hour and a half of music, plus a 20-minute break. Additionally, hearing classical music in an elevator or at a mall does not diminish its quality. I do believe you have a point with your second statement. The idea of trying to recreate a Mozart concert exactly as it was in the 1780s is anachronistic. However, your take on improvisation is simply wrong. Many musicians do improvise; the issues are that they are often not educated specifically in the style they are playing, and the sheet music editions are not always up to date. Performance practices change over time, and not every musician can be educated in the writing style of Baroque composers like Vivaldi. Performance changed drastically with composers like Brahms and especially Mahler, who wrote down everything exactly as they wanted it, making it unnecessary for musicians to learn improvisational traditions—but so much was implied rather than written that the performance tradition was lost. It is up to experts to understand these practices and convey them to musicians. The problem with "museumification" isn't that the music is taught as a thing of the past, but that it is performed as such. In the 19th century, the standard classical repertoire was slowly formed—before that, almost only contemporary music was played—and we have been stuck with the music of the past since then. I am not complaining, as I love old music, but we should focus more on what happened after composers like Stravinsky and Schoenberg. Here are some examples of contemporary music that demonstrate how vastly different the field is: Ryuichi Sakamoto’s "Bibo No Aozora" (1996), which was later used in the film Babel; Steve Reich’s "Radio Rewrite" (2013), where one of the great American composers wrote a piece inspired by Radiohead; Simon Steen-Andersen’s Praesens (circa 2006), which draws inspiration from the sound of the 1940s and 50s; Eric Whitacre’s "Alleluia" (2012), which reflects a trend in modern choral music to blend influences from the 15th, 16th, and 19th centuries; and Arvo Pärt’s Symphony No. 3 (1971), which is one of my favorite pieces. TL;DR: I think the original poster is wrong and did not research the subject.
label: critique

text: Honestly, this is survivorship bias all the way down. Art has always been a rich kid’s game, going back centuries: Mozart’s dad was a court musician, Chopin grew up around Polish aristocracy, and the Impressionists mostly had family money or patrons. The romantic image of the working-class musician kicking down the door was real for a small window, mostly in post-war America and Britain, but it was never the historical norm. It was the exception that everyone now mistakes for the rule. The reason you remember more lower- or middle-class musicians from the 1960s to the 2010s is that you only saw the ones who made it. You didn't see the thousands of poor kids who picked up a guitar at 16, played in garages for three years, ran out of money, had a kid, or got a real job at 24 and disappeared. Meanwhile, the rich kid down the street kept going for another decade because his parents could float him. It is not that wealthy families produce more talent; they produce more endurance. The runway is the entire game. And here is the part that is actually counterintuitive: the technical barrier to making music has collapsed. A teenager with a $400 laptop and a free DAW can make something that would have required a $200,000 studio in 1985. By every objective measure, poor kids have more access to music production than at any point in human history. So why does it feel like rich kids dominate? Because the bottleneck moved. It is no longer 'can you afford the equipment,' it is 'can you afford to do this for ten years without it paying you.' And that part has not changed; it has probably gotten worse, since the streaming economy basically guarantees you will be broke for longer. I would push back on the Rick Beato framing a bit. When he says musicians come from wealthy families now, what he is really observing is that the people who can survive the music industry's economic structure long enough to become visible are disproportionately those with safety nets. That was always true. We just had a brief romantic period where it did not feel that way. Anyway, you would need an actual large-sample study to know what is really going on, and I do not think anyone has done it. Everything we are saying, including this comment, is just looking at the tip of the iceberg.
label: critique

text: While I do not disagree with you—it is nearly unheard of for a band to reach Beyoncé levels this way—variants of this have happened over the years. The band Boston is probably the most famous example. The group's debut album was solely a studio project, without a real band attached to it, when guitarist and songwriter Tom Scholz began creating the record in his basement studio. If I remember correctly, they held the record for the highest-charting debut from an artist until Guns N' Roses broke that record years later, but they had never played a gig. They were, however, signed to a major label. There have been other groups that have followed similar paths, having no live gigging at all before making it big. Often these bands, though, are really studio projects with a singular leader hiring other players to round out the work, and they usually secure a major label deal before their success happens. Many times with projects like these, the group in question typically has some sort of connection to the industry. Toto was a band of nothing but successful session musicians who were already well-respected in the industry, so it was sort of easy for them to secure a major label deal without touring. Throughout pop music history, there have been numerous one-off projects assembled by record producers for the sole purpose of recording a particular song. This was especially prevalent in the 60s with a bunch of bubblegum singles; The Archies' 'Sugar, Sugar'—that wasn't a real band, for example, just session guys playing a song the producer wrote. Kim Fowley did this a lot, and the whole Kasenetz-Katz factory of bubblegum records for Buddah Records is also a good example. None of these bands toured until they had a hit, and even then, the group on the road were just hired musicians, not part of an artistic collective. They were just guys on salary playing the hit song around the country at various DJ dances. There have been several instances, however, of artists signed to indie labels hitting it big without the assistance of a major label. In fact, this is becoming more and more common today as artists use the internet to build a buzz. Most of these artists do tour, though. So, it can and has happened, but I would say that the consensus here is correct: it is by no means common. Possible? Yeah.
label: critique

text: In 1997, Russian artists Komar & Melamid surveyed people on what they most liked and disliked in music, and then using those criteria created the “Most Wanted Song” and “Most Unwanted Song”. Ironically, neither of those titles turned out to be accurate. The Most Wanted Song contains electric guitar, piano, saxophone and lyrics about love, while the Most Unwanted Song fills out its 22 minute runtime with bagpipes, a rapping opera singer and a children’s choir advertising Walmart. Go and listen to them, but before you do, ask yourself: which one will you still remember a week from now? Which one will you tell your friends about? Which one do you think has 6x more Spotify plays than the other?Both of these songs are interesting given the context in which they were created, but divorced from this context, one of them ceases to be remarkable or noteworthy in any way. The Most Wanted Song is simply an accumulation of agreeable components, being completely vapid in itself. Despite being deliberately unpleasant in as many ways as possible, the Most Unwanted Song is utterly unique, vastly more memorable and probably much more meaningful to the people who hear it than its supposedly perfect counterpart will ever be. I feel like there’s something quite profound in that.
label: critique

text: I think that hip-hop will probably follow a similar trajectory to jazz in the next few decades. The mass popularity will fade as style moves on, but it will have a very dedicated following of people into the cerebral, experimental nature, musicianship, skill, and craft of it. The same way jazz stuck around because you will find the best instrumental improvisers in that genre, and classical music stuck around because you will find the best technical performers of written music in that genre, I believe hip-hop will stick around because you will find the best free-form spoken-word poets still working in that genre. Just like people still go to jazz and classical to marvel at the skill, they will come back to hip-hop for the same reason. Rock, on the other hand, I am not so sure about. I think it is more likely than hip-hop or jazz to dwindle out because it has always been, in general, more focused on style and less on skill. It seems like idioms that invent a new style without requiring too much skill tend to fade. I am not badmouthing it—just an observation. Obviously, all music takes skill, but with other genres that had a quicker shelf life, like disco, new wave, and dubstep, the focus was all on creating a new sound that had not been heard before, not on wowing anyone with technical proficiency. Once people get over the novelty of a new style, it falls off pretty quickly. Rock stayed relevant so long by radically reinventing its style every decade. If you show an alien Little Richard, then Pink Floyd, then Lamb of God, I do not think it would say they all sound like the same genre. But if you play Miles Davis, then Herbie Hancock, then Esperanza Spalding, there is a lot more continuity. The point is, rock has to invent radically new sounds to stay relevant where jazz, classical, and hip-hop do not need to. If there is some new subgenre that branches out and a new sound appears, I think that is how rock stays popular. I could also see the subgenres that are as highly technical as jazz, classical, and hip-hop getting a bit more staying power. I think metal, with all its subgenres and its technicality, is the branch of rock that has the highest chance of staying alive indefinitely and following a similar trajectory to jazz and classical.
label: critique

text: It’s complicated. On one hand, there are a lot of terrible critics out there, people who are not as well informed as they probably should be, and this has accelerated in recent years with the opinion-industrial complex that is social media. Now anyone can have an opinion, share an opinion and even occasionally people will pay attention and more extreme views tend to garner more attention and therefore more money. The standard of criticism on average probably isn’t as good as it’s ever been. On the other hand, there is a strain of anti-critic opinion that seems to be entirely dismissive of music criticism, because music preference is subjective (which it is). However, that reasonable observation seems to have gradually evolved into an unreasonable one: that critics have nothing to add or tell us about art. ‘If people like it, it is good’ is not necessarily wrong, as much as it is conflating two truths that don’t fit together. Personal preference at one end, artistic merit, ambition, effort, mastery at the other. In the middle of this all is a general lack of consensus on what the job of a critic actually is. Criticism in the old school literary sense is more like a point-of-view, it’s an analysis from a specific perspective. That’s why you have feminist criticism, Marxist criticism, critical race theory etc. it’s basically ‘what does this work of art mean, when looked at through this lens?’. Note that is not the same as saying that every piece of art is intentionally feminist, or Marxist or whatever, just that a lot of art can be understood in different, sometimes enlightening ways. However, much modern criticism (not just in music) is now largely a matter of recommendation. You should listen to this, you shouldn’t watch that, thumbs up, thumbs down. So non-critics can be forgiven for thinking that subjectivity is the only relevant standard. Critics have inadvertently undermined the value of their insight by only offering endorsement or disapproval and not much else. At the risk of stating the obvious, criticism can only be saved if critics are able to give people insight, perspective and context on a piece of art beyond what is already widely known. As much as any piece of art can be recommended, it should be before how thematically, aesthetically, narratively interesting or ambitious it is rather than how ‘good’ or ‘bad’ it is. There are critics who know a lot about music, and have a lot to offer, but have to focus on that.
label: critique

text: I have many thoughts on this but for me, you can't discount that the way music is consumed now is completely different. The Billboard charts originated in the 40s, as I recall, but its cultural importance really took off with the rise of the teenager. Here was a market of young people, young people who were earning money much earlier than later generations would, and they could spare a bit of cash for a 45" single. They probably weren't flush, but they had enough for that. They were the market that drove chart-hit culture. And as long as there were singles made of vinyl, that was the case, well up to when CDs became a thing. It bred an entire cultural focus on popular music that sold enough to make a chart entry, mostly driven by each generation of young people. We could discuss the whole "long player" phenomenon that arrived in the mid-60s and flourished in the 70s with the rise of prog, which was a big factor in CD sales and the slowdown of the singles market, but I digress. Fast forward to en masse streaming, and the market now includes much older people. Previously, when you "aged out" of the popular music demographic, you would probably be listening to "Hey Jude" in the comfort of your own living room and nobody would know. You bought the record years ago, so you listening to it on your record player isn't going to inform anyone that you like it and you listen to it on repeat. And if it wears out, you'll probably go and purchase an LP with it on. That one purchase alone doesn't influence popular culture, unless you happen to buy it at a time that featured it in a film or something else with popular appeal. Now, quite a large number of people have Spotify accounts and can listen to what they want, when they want, and what they listen to can have an impact that it didn't previously. If you're older, you can stream 80s 2 Tone music exclusively and it's logged now. Chances are, if you have any offspring, they might well stream it too because they've grown up with it, so even if you're a young person listening to new music, you might still be streaming a bit of musical comfort music too. Whereas, back in the days of vinyl etc., you would just borrow your parent's copy of "Hey Jude" if you wanted to listen to it. There's probably lots of factors that play into why modern music isn't as popular, but I don't think you can discount the impact of the change in how we listen to music and how that has changed the market for music.
label: critique

text: If you read through Pitchfork's Top 100 Songs of 2021, it's kind of funny how most of it is just songs ripped straight off the radio. Fantano, The Grammys, The Guardian and Rolling Stone are doing it too. Pop hits by Doja Cat, Lil Nas X, Billie Eilish, Charli XCX, Justin Bieber, Adele, Olivia Rodrigo, Lana Del Rey and more. Don't get me wrong, I'm a huge pop fan! And these are good artists. But if you go back to major critics' late 2000s and early 2010s year-end lists you'll notice there was much more of a focus on giving credit to creative, independent artists who thought outside the box and who truly created the most novel and noteworthy songs of the year. Sure you might have had a Lady Gaga or Taylor Swift song here and there, but these days, Year-End lists read like I'm just looking down the Billboard Hot 100 chart. As someone who looks towards Year-End Lists as a tool to find the most unique, exciting and cutting-edge music of the year, this is obviously a disappointment. I expect more from a professional music publication listening to 100s and 1000s of the albums and songs a year, collectively. If even after listening to all those projects, you still come to the conclusion that Lil Nas X and Olivia Rodrigo are this generation's Pink Floyd, then man are we in a sad time for music criticism. It also kind of amazes me that all of the amazing pop music that was coming out in the late 2000s and early 2010s got almost no clout or attention by critics, when lately it seems like whatever the hottest, latest pop album that a major star releases will not only get coverage by all the major critics and publications, but a pretty decent score, too. Pitchfork never even reviewed most of the artists making the biggest (and in my opinion best) hit pop songs and albums of the late 2000s/early 2010s. Imagine critics in 2009 putting Rokstarr by Taio Cruz as one of the best albums of the year? Again, that's a great pop album by an artist I really like, but to put that over a Flaming Lips, Animal Collective, Phoenix or the xx record from that year would seem crazy. How about Night Shades by Cobra Starship? Ocean Eyes by Owl City? These were all some of the biggest pop albums of their era and created some of the biggest hits, too. But you didn't see these artists on every critic and publication's year-end lists at the end of the year. In fact, most of them weren't even reviewed. Why is this? Do critics think that this era of pop is just inherently better and more respectable? Because I have to disagree with that, too. The pop of the late 2000s/early 2010s era was great for the same reasons 1980s pop is so great. It was simply fun, pure pop music. It didn't attempt to be anything more than that, which is perfectly okay, preferable in fact. I love pop music that I can dance to and forget about the world for 3 minutes and 50 seconds. And that's something I can't do as much with recent stuff. You could argue Eilish is more artistic, Rodrigo more versatile and Lil Nas X more revolutionary. But when it comes to pop music being POP MUSIC, I feel that propping up the current Billboard fare as the greatest musical achievements of the year is questionable at best and lazy at worst. If you're reading this Pitchfork writers and modern critics, please, please dig deeper.
label: criticism

text: Songwriting credits are vague and tricky at that top level of the industry, they just don't really tell you much about the actual creative process that went into a song from start to finish. There are professional and financial considerations to crediting songwriters which might not have anything to do with the actual creative contributions. With Beyonce in particular, I would be willing to bet that she at least initiated the concepts of some of her biggest songs even if she ended up having very little involvement in directly writing or producing them. Or, she may have had quite a heavy hand in the writing but allowed the other songwriters to take all the credit as a form of compensation or professional courtesy. But even if she is selling the public absolutely false and completely fabricated stories about how she single-handedly wrote her best songs - who cares? A pop star as massive as her is supposed to be mythologized like that. If you care enough about pop music to care about songwriting credits, you should also be aware of how the pop artist as a whole is the end-product - their personality, their image, their stories, etc., are all generated collaboratively and are all designed to sell. Once you realize that, you can engage with the pop artist critically on that level and focus on whether the fiction of the pop artist is interesting and engaging, rather than judging how authentic the pop artist actually is in reality.
label: critique

text: That’s definitely true, but a lot of producers are actually making their songs with less volume contrast and louder, making it physically feel different when listening. They also use less spatial style mixing, so it’s more centered and the sound doesn’t “travel” around your headphones.  It’s to accommodate the smaller speakers people are listening on and streaming compression, so that it still sounds virtually the same no matter how you’re listening. They amp up the mid range and vocals, and the low end depth and high end air portions are usually trimmed. Not to mention the point that people usually make which is a ton of auto tune and the lack of real instruments. But even then early 2000s pop, which was designed more for your car speakers or radio, still has auto tune and electronic beats but it feels different when listening to it. It’s not just nostalgia.

text: For me, music has always been more than just sound in the background, it’s about depth, unpredictability and the effort an artist puts in to create something that stays with you. Even if a track wasn’t in my usual taste, as long as it carried some soul, layers or creativity, I could respect and enjoy it.But when I listen to a lot of today’s mainstream music, especially pop and hip-hop, I honestly feel let down. So much of it sounds formulaic. Same beats, recycled lyrics, predictable drops. It often feels like it’s made just to go viral on reels or TikTok rather than to stand as music on its own. That frustrates me, because it reduces music to just another consumable product rather than an art form.I know some people enjoy simplicity and catchiness, and that’s totally fine. But personally, I find it harder and harder to connect with what’s popular today. It feels shallow, almost like the depth and effort have been stripped away in exchange for short-term attention. text: There are actually a couple studies that back up the idea that pop has gotten "worse" over time... One was a meta analysis of something like almost a half million songs from 1955-2010 done by the Spanish National Research Council... They ran all these songs through some algorithms to look at harmonic complexity, timbral diversity and loudness... The study found that, since the ‘50s, there has been a decrease not only in the diversity of chords in a given song, but also in the number of novel transitions... All these comments about "survivorship bias" are a huge oversimplification. The musical landscape is drastically different today, especially since all the studio mega mergers of the late 90s and the death of MTV.

text: I disagree with many of the commenters on here. I don’t think it’s just an age thing. I think the big thing is the change in technology allowed larger jumps in music than ever before. More or less, music was similar from like 1500-1870. Then came the blues, jazz, early rock n roll etc. those genres were immense jumps from the prior decades that made in my opinion music a fundamentally different listening experience than what existed before. Those rough genres dominated music until like the mid 70’s. Punk, new wave, hip hop, came along. Once again a big shift in a short period. Now compare that to todays music. If my grandfather grew up listening to miles Davis, Frank Sinatra, and say the Beatles, how does what Kanye west do scratch a similar musical itch? It doesn’t, it’s a different language essentially. My father grew up with 80’s rock that while different, it still spoke essentially the same language as those before it.People are quick to just say because boomers do and think xyz. But the truth is our society as a whole has never changed faster than it has in the last 100 years. And the boomers were in the middle. The music of the boomer is typically mentioned as classic rock, wouldn’t you say that classic rock is much more similar to big band, jazz, and other pop music from the 1910’s-1950’s than the type of stuff that can be heard today?I believe that if I took someone from 1750, and played them a select few songs by the Beatles that they could understand at least a little bit of what’s going on if not more. But if I played Travis Scott it would largely be not understood. Music I believe used to be more universal and timeless because it was indebted to older musical forms. New stuff today is much more contextual and niche (not necessarily bad in any sense). It’s also important to note that the lyrical content of music today reflects how people live and talk today. We talk about mental illness, sexuality, drugs, and other topics that were not talked about openly before. So the lyrical content probably tackles stuff that older generations don’t relate to because for them you didn’t talk that way.Yes I’m painting in broad strokes and my details could be a little off. But I do think it’s the pace of technological and societal change that creates significant leaps in musical language that tend to lock people in to certain areas. My great grandparents could at least somewhat get the Beatles, as could kids today. But could they get and empathize with mumble rap and death metal? Unless they are extremely open minded, I doubt it.

text: I agree with your statement overall.
