text: I think there's a two primary factors:1) Technology has developed to a point where it can seamlessly and consistently reinforce w/e musical preferences it thinks you have, which creates a feedback loop. Prior to the widespread availability of recorded music, the only way you'd ever experience music was in its performance. In the 1800s people like Chopin and especially Liszt were major celebrities who went on concert tours like today. You could read accounts of their performances, but you had to be there, and every performance is going to be at least slightly different. There's plenty of people who were hugely famous then who are quite obscure now because they didn't compose as much compared to performing, which they did at a high level but left no direct record of. Likewise music at church, or your various local songs and dances, etc. There's a major impermanence to it all: it happens at a specific time and place, then it's just gone.You develop a way to record, and especially a way to cheaply do so and make it widely available, and now not only do more people have more access to music regularly, but the music itself becomes fixed in place. The content and especially the listening experience can be duplicated. I can describe a particularly amazing solo at a concert and evoke the experience; if it was recorded I can hear the exact solo over and over, and have that experience. In the studio you can fine tune things, redo them, combine individual moments and make a "perfect" listening experience, or at least a consistent one. That's where I think the 'synapses form in your brain and lock you in' really comes into play, especially when talking about Boomers and whatnot. Cuz I think that editing and adding effects, enhancements, etc makes that dopamine hit even more powerful. And while records are ubiquitous and some people had large collections, it still requires going out and getting them. There's a level of manual curation required, and for many older people, they love certain groups because those were the primary record/tape/CD (of likely few) they actually owned. My dad had a couple Beatles cassets that he played often in the car; it's great music imo, but by virtue of those circumstances I also listened to it constantly throughout my childhood. It's what was readily available.In our contemporary times, we very easily have access to pretty much any and all music that's been digitized and put somewhere online. But we also have powerful tools of analysis that break down various elements of music and can find other music that matches those elements. So even though we're getting exposed to more music quantitatively, it's still falling within those confines. It's new, but still familiar. It's also automatic and ubiquitous. 2) Music is a commodity whose purpose is generating profit.Propelling those technological advances is the fact that music is produced in a context where the only thing that truly matters is ROI. Therefore it's imperative to figure out what is or is going to be profitable and maximize that until it's tapped out. And it's similarly vital to reduce the cost of production as much as possible.That's always undergirded music, but the predictive ability of what wld be popular wasn't as seemingly fine tuned as it is now, which I think left room for more experimentation. Frank Zappa has a quote about stodgy old conservative record execs being much more open to giving weird stuff a chance so long as it was cheap to make, compared to the hip progressive record execs in the 80s who were certain they knew what was cool/profitable and ironically made them even more conservative and basically hostile to 'new' stuff. (Acknowledging the following is purely subjective lol) With those same powerful tools of analysis, and especially the vast amount of available music, that same dynamic seems to be in overdrive now. It makes newer contemporary popular music feel hollow and ephemeral. It starts to feel like it all sounds the same because the powers that be have influenced technology to intentionally try to make it all sound kinda the same, and have the means to do so because of that profit motive. It incentivizes putting out as much stuff as often as possible, which further incentivizes not trying to experiment too much and try to match algorithmic expectations. Like pretty much everything else in contemporary life, we're being ruled by spreadsheets.That's not to say that it sounds bad -- quite the opposite. It's all very well produced. That's not to say it has no merit and it's quantifiably worse than older music or w/e. Nor that putting out a ton of stuff consistently somehow invalidates it. There are people out there who post things almost daily and it's all fabulous stuff. But I think when people reflexively dismiss contemporary pop music or "new" music in general, what they're really unhappy about is that profit generating machine coming to the forefront instead of whirring in the background. In the good ole days there was at least the facade of the musician as an artist, which is now replaced with musician as a brand. It was always so, but now it's not just blatant, but celebrated, which for most people feels very wrong (and imo it IS wrong and ultimately a misuse of incredible technology).There's obviously various exceptions and counter examples to this - but I think this works as an overall framework.
label: critique

text: In my case, I find a lot of newer pop music to be overstimulating. People can design music now so that every synth, every hit of a drum machine, or every crash effect going into a chorus is as attention-grabbing as it can possibly be. I personally find it grating.I also find it harder to truly love music that isn’t played on instruments in real time. I can appreciate how people compose EDM or pop songs on a conceptual level, but being able to play in time and create a groove is what gives music its soul. There’s a time and place for processed junk food, but at the end of the day, real natural ingredients are what really satisfy you.I’m 30. I grew up listening to a lot of my dad’s music from the 60s and 70s, but I also heard plenty of other stuff. 1970s music is still the most satisfying music to me (as well as some modern stuff, often indie or indie-adjacent, that uses those same production approaches).
label: critique

text: I love the point about context. To older ears, modern popular music sounds totally formless, but it actually has highly specific formulas—they just aren’t the formulas of the 70s or 80s.Look at the decline of the traditional, soaring vocal chorus. So much of modern pop and hip-hop relies on the 'drop chorus' or the 'anti-chorus,' where the energy actually dips, or the vocal melody is replaced entirely by a repetitive instrumental loop or production gimmick. If you grew up expecting a massive, harmonic payoff in a chorus, modern songwriting feels deeply unsatisfying and lazy.There’s also the 'TikTok effect' on songwriting structures. Songs are getting shorter because streaming algorithms reward skips less than complete plays, and tracks are literally engineered around a single 15-second snippet designed to go viral. The verse, the bridge, the intro—they're all treated as filler surrounding that one tiny part. It’s a totally different approach to composition where the song is just a delivery vehicle for a digital asset.
label: critique

text: The point about remote recording and mixing is spot-on, but I think the issue with modern production goes even deeper into how the tracks are structurally and sonically engineered.First, from a structural standpoint, the compression of song lengths for the streaming/TikTok era has completely ruined the narrative of a song. When a track is forced to be under 2 minutes and 30 seconds to maximize repeat plays and algorithm scores, you have to sacrifice the intro, the bridge, and any sense of gradual build-up. Songs just start abruptly at full volume, hit a quick verse-chorus-verse-chorus loop, and vanish. There’s no room for tension and release, which is the literal foundation of musical emotional resonance.Second, on a technical mix level, the post-COVID shift to artists recording their parts remotely in random home setups has created a massive headache for mixing engineers. When you have three different features on a track recorded on three different microphones in untreated rooms, the engineer can’t easily blend them into a cohesive acoustic space. To fix this, they are forced to use heavy digital processing—surgical EQ, aggressive tuning, and massive amounts of dynamic compression—just to make the vocals sound like they belong in the same universe.The result is a mix that feels completely flat, static, and lacking in depth. Every element is smashed forward against the 'glass' of the speaker at the exact same perceived volume. It’s physically exhausting to listen to for long periods because there are no micro-dynamics left. It’s not just a lack of talent; it’s that the actual physics and economics of modern music delivery are actively hostile to good audio engineering.
label: critique

text: Keeping the Canadian theme rolling here, I’m fascinated by the compilation Big Shiny Tunes 2 released in Canada at the end of 1997. The entire series was successful, but it’s Big Shiny Tunes 2 in particular that became part of the zeitgeist. It ultimately sold 1.2 million copies in Canada, which may not seem like a lot, but that accounts for 4% of the population of Canada at the time. It would be the equivalent of 10M units sold in the United States.
